results.question: How many wounds did Julius Caesar receive when he was assassinated?
results.ideal_answer: According to Suetonius, Caesar was stabbed with twenty-three wounds. He uttered a groan at the first wound but no cry, though some report that he said "And you too, my son?" to Marcus Brutus.
results.answer: According to Suetonius, Julius Caesar was stabbed with three and twenty (23) wounds [1].

results.question: According to Herodotus, what did Egyptian households do when a cat died a natural death?
results.ideal_answer: According to Herodotus, everyone living in a house where a cat died a natural death shaved their eyebrows — only their eyebrows, whereas when a dog died they shaved their whole body and head.
results.answer: According to Herodotus, when a cat died a natural death in an Egyptian household, all those who dwelt in that house shaved their eyebrows only [1][2]. He contrasts this with the death of a dog, where the household would shave their whole body and head.

results.question: What period of history did Gibbon declare the happiest and most prosperous for the human race?
results.ideal_answer: Gibbon wrote that if asked to fix the period during which the condition of the human race was most happy and prosperous, one would without hesitation name the span from the death of Domitian to the accession of Commodus — the era of Nerva, Trajan, Hadrian, and the Antonines.
results.answer: Edward Gibbon declared that the period from the death of Domitian to the accession of Commodus was the happiest and most prosperous for the human race. He wrote: "If a man were called to fix the period in the history of the world, during which the condition of the human race was most happy and prosperous, he would, without hesitation, name that which elapsed from the death of Domitian to the accession of Commodus" [15]. This period (AD 96–180) encompassed the reigns of Nerva, Trajan, Hadrian, and the two Antonines (Antoninus Pius and Marcus Aurelius).

results.question: According to Tacitus, what did the Germans consider the greatest disgrace in battle?
results.ideal_answer: Tacitus says the greatest disgrace that could befall a German warrior was to have abandoned his shield. He adds that for a chief's companions it was lifelong reproach and infamy to retreat from the field surviving their chief.
results.answer: According to Tacitus, the Germans considered abandoning their shields to be the greatest disgrace in battle. He writes: 'The greatest disgrace that can befall them is to have abandoned their shields.' A person branded with this ignominy was excluded from religious rites and assemblies, and many who survived battle chose to end their infamy by hanging themselves [1].

results.question: Whom did the Athenians send as a runner to Sparta before the battle of Marathon, and what happened to him on the way?
results.ideal_answer: The Athenian generals sent Pheidippides, a professional long-distance runner, as herald to Sparta. On the way, by Mount Parthenion above Tegea, the god Pan met him, called him by name, and bade him ask the Athenians why they paid him no honor although he was well disposed to them and had often been — and would again be — serviceable.
results.answer: The Athenians sent Pheidippides, an Athenian professional long-distance runner, as a herald to Sparta before the battle of Marathon [1]. On his way, near Mount Parthenion above Tegea, the god Pan met him, called out his name, and told him to ask the Athenians why they had no care for him, though he was well-disposed toward them and had been helpful on many occasions before and would be so again [1]. Pheidippides himself reported this encounter to the Athenians, and they believed the tale to be true [1].

results.question: What epitaph was inscribed for the Spartans who fell at Thermopylae?
results.ideal_answer: The inscription for the Spartans read (in Macaulay's translation): "Stranger, report this word, we pray, to the Spartans, that lying here in this spot we remain, faithfully keeping their laws."
results.answer: The epitaph inscribed for the Spartans who fell at Thermopylae reads: "Stranger, report this word, we pray, to the Spartans, that lying here in this spot we remain, faithfully keeping their laws" [3]. George Grote renders it as: "Stranger, tell the Lacedæmonians, that we lie here, in obedience to their orders" [1]. Both are translations of the same Greek inscription, which Herodotus records was set up by the Amphictyons specifically for the Spartan dead, separate from the general inscription for all the Peloponnesian fallen [3].

results.question: What was Augustus' boast about the city of Rome and what he did for it?
results.ideal_answer: Augustus so improved the city — which had not been built in a manner suited to the grandeur of the empire — that he boasted, in Suetonius' words, that he "found it of brick, but left it of marble."
results.answer: Augustus boasted that he found Rome a city of bricks and left it a city of marble. Suetonius records that under his administration the city was so much improved that "he boasted, not without reason, that he 'found it of brick, but left it of marble'" [3]. A footnote in the same work notes that Dio offers an alternative version: "That Rome, which I found built of mud, I shall leave you firm as a rock" [1].

results.question: What honor did Caligula plan to give his horse Incitatus?
results.ideal_answer: Suetonius reports it was said Caligula intended to make his horse Incitatus consul. He had already given the horse a marble stable, an ivory manger, purple housings, a jewelled frontlet, and a house with slaves and furniture for entertaining guests invited in the horse's name.
results.answer: Caligula planned to make his horse Incitatus a **consul**. Suetonius writes that "it is even said that he intended to make him consul" [1]. Cassius Dio similarly reports that Caligula "promised... that he would appoint him consul" and would certainly have done so had he lived longer [2]. The secondary historian William Smith also notes that Caligula "even conferred the consulship on his favorite horse" [4].

results.question: How does Caesar describe the division of Gaul at the start of his commentaries?
results.ideal_answer: Caesar opens by stating that all Gaul is divided into three parts, one inhabited by the Belgae, another by the Aquitani, and the third by the people called Celts in their own language and Gauls in Latin — all differing from each other in language, customs, and laws.
results.answer: At the opening of his Commentaries on the Gallic War (Book I, Chapter 1), Caesar writes: "All Gaul is divided into three parts, one of which the Belgae inhabit, the Aquitani another, those who in their own language are called Celts, in ours Gauls, the third." He adds that all three differ from each other in language, customs, and laws, and identifies the rivers that separate them: the Garonne separates the Gauls from the Aquitani, and the Marne and the Seine separate them from the Belgae [2]. He also notes that among the three, the Belgae are the bravest, being farthest from Roman civilisation and nearest to the Germans across the Rhine [2].

results.question: What answer did the Delphic oracle give Croesus when he asked whether to attack Persia?
results.ideal_answer: The oracles answered that if Croesus marched against the Persians he would destroy a great empire, and counselled him to find the most powerful of the Hellenes and make them his friends. The prophecy was ambiguous — the great empire he destroyed turned out to be his own.
results.answer: The Delphic oracle told Croesus that if he marched against the Persians, he would destroy a great empire [1]. Croesus interpreted this as a promise of success against Cyrus, but the oracle was deliberately ambiguous — the "great empire" he destroyed turned out to be his own. After his defeat, the Pythian priestess later defended the oracle by saying Croesus should have asked for clarification about which empire was meant [4].

results.question: What were Nero's last words about himself as he prepared to die?
results.ideal_answer: As his grave was prepared before his eyes, Nero wept and repeatedly said, "What an artist is now about to perish!"
results.answer: As Nero prepared to take his own life, he lamented his own artistic talents being lost. Suetonius records him repeatedly saying, "What an artist is now about to perish!" [1]. Cassius Dio reports a similar version: "Jupiter, what an artist perishes in me!" [6].

results.question: How many Jews perished and how many were taken captive during the whole siege of Jerusalem in the Jewish War?
results.ideal_answer: Josephus reports that the number carried captive during the whole war was ninety-seven thousand, and the number who perished during the whole siege was eleven hundred thousand (1,100,000) — most of them Jews who had come up from the country to Jerusalem for the feast of unleavened bread and were then shut in by the Roman army, destroyed by pestilence and famine.
results.answer: According to Josephus, during the whole siege of Jerusalem, 1,100,000 (eleven hundred thousand) Jews perished, and 97,000 (ninety-seven thousand) were taken captive [1].
results.judge_notes: faithfulness: The specific casualty figures and attribution to Josephus are explicitly stated in the cited source passage [1], with no invented or unsupported content. | completeness: The answer clearly states both numbers requested—1,100,000 perished and 97,000 taken captive—so it fully covers everything the question asks without requiring the additional context in the reference. | attribution: The sources agree on the figures provided by Josephus for the siege of Jerusalem, and the answer correctly attributes the information to him without any misattribution.

results.question: What did Eleazar urge the defenders of Masada to do rather than fall into Roman hands?
results.ideal_answer: Eleazar urged the defenders to die free by their own hands rather than be taken — to slay their own wives and children and then one another, having first burned their possessions and the fortress, so as to preserve their freedom and deny the Romans the pleasure of capturing them. They carried out the mass suicide, sparing only their stores of food.
results.answer: Eleazar urged the defenders of Masada to kill their wives and children and then commit mass suicide rather than fall into Roman hands. In his speech, he exhorted them: "Let our wives die before they are abused, and our children before they have tasted of slavery; and after we have slain them, let us bestow that glorious benefit upon one another mutually, and preserve ourselves in freedom, as an excellent funeral monument for us" [2]. He also urged them to destroy their money and the fortress by fire, sparing only their provisions as a testimony that they were not subdued for want of necessities but had chosen death before slavery [2]. The great majority of the defenders were prevailed upon and carried out this mass suicide, with only two women and five children surviving by hiding in underground caverns [1][3].
results.judge_notes: faithfulness: Every claim in the answer is directly supported by the provided source passages, including Eleazar's exhortation to kill their families and themselves, destroy the fortress and money, and the survival of only two women and five children. | completeness: The answer fully covers everything the question asks, including Eleazar's urging to kill their wives and children, commit mutual suicide, burn their possessions and the fortress, and spare only their food stores, with extra detail not penalized. | attribution: The sources agree on the details of Eleazar's speech and the outcome at Masada, and the answer correctly attributes the specific claims and quotes to the relevant passages without any misattribution.

results.question: What act of a starving mother during the famine in Jerusalem does Josephus describe in the Wars of the Jews?
results.ideal_answer: Josephus describes Mary of Bethezuba, a once-wealthy woman driven to madness by the famine, who killed her infant son, roasted him, ate one half and kept the other half concealed — an atrocity he says had no parallel in Greek or barbarian history.
results.answer: Josephus relates that a woman named Mary, the daughter of Eleazar from the village of Bethezob, during the famine in besieged Jerusalem, “snatching up her son, who was a child sucking at her breast … slew her son, and then roasted him, and eat the one half of him, and kept the other half by her concealed” [3]. When the seditious men later entered her house, drawn by the smell of cooking, she uncovered what remained of her son and said to them, “This is mine own son, and what hath been done was mine own doing! Come, eat of this food; for I have eaten of it myself!” [1].

results.question: By what act could a man become the priest of Diana's grove at Nemi, and what was the priest called?
results.ideal_answer: A man could win the priesthood only by breaking off a bough of a certain sacred tree in the grove and then slaying the reigning priest in single combat; having killed him, he succeeded to the office and held it until he in turn was slain by a stronger or craftier challenger. The priest bore the title King of the Wood (Rex Nemorensis).
results.answer: According to Frazer, a runaway slave could break off a bough from a certain sacred tree in the grove of Diana at Nemi. results.question: What was the supreme or national god of the Assyrians?
results.ideal_answer: The supreme, distinctively national god of the Assyrians was Asshur — head of the Assyrian pantheon, styled "the great Lord" and "King of all the Gods," the tutelary deity of the kings and of the country, whose name was identical with that of Assyria itself.
results.answer: The supreme national god of the Assyrians was Asshur. According to George Rawlinson, Asshur stood "at the head of the Assyrian Pantheon" and was called "the great Lord," "the King of all the Gods," and "he who rules supreme over the Gods" [1]. He was regarded as the especial tutelary deity of both the kings and the country: the Assyrian kings described themselves as "the servants of Asshur," their enemies as "the enemies of Asshur," and the Assyrian religion itself as "the worship of Asshur" [2]. No other deity received such national pre-eminence in Assyria.
results.judge_notes: faithfulness: Every claim in the answer is directly and accurately supported by the cited source passages, with no invented or unsupported content. | completeness: The answer fully identifies Asshur as the supreme national god and comprehensively covers all aspects the question asks for, including his supremacy over the pantheon and his national role as tutelary deity. | attribution: The sources unanimously agree that Asshur was the supreme national god, and the answer correctly attributes this information to George Rawlinson without any misattribution.

results.question: What was the principal building material of Chaldaea, and why was stone so little used?
results.ideal_answer: The principal building material of Chaldaea was brick, made from the abundant clay of the alluvial plain (sun-dried, or kiln-baked for greater hardness). Stone was scarcely used because the alluvium was destitute of it — any stone had to be imported from adjacent countries — so the Chaldaeans built with clay brick in its default.
results.answer: The principal building material of Chaldaea was brick, made from the abundant and excellent clay of the alluvial plain [1][3]. Stone was so little used because Chaldaea, located entirely on alluvium, was "wholly destitute" of stone; it had to be imported from adjacent regions at considerable difficulty, and such importation was "very limited" [1]. Rawlinson notes that "nowhere within the limits of the alluvium was a quarry to be found" and that transporting stone across the soft, oozy soil would have prevented its general adoption [3].
results.judge_notes: faithfulness: Every claim is directly supported by the cited passages, including the dominance of brick, the lack of local stone, and the transport difficulties across soft alluvium. | completeness: The answer correctly identifies brick as the principal building material and fully explains that stone was scarcely used because the alluvial plain was destitute of stone and importing it was difficult and limited. | attribution: The sources agree on the principal building material and the reasons for the scarcity of stone, and the answer correctly attributes the information to the relevant passages and author without any misattribution.

results.question: How many men were appointed to draw up the laws at Athens and became known as the Thirty?
results.ideal_answer: Thirty men were chosen — the body that became known as the Thirty (later the Thirty Tyrants) — appointed to draft a constitution based on the ancestral laws of Athens, though they never published the laws and instead seized power for themselves.
results.answer: Thirty men were appointed to draw up the laws at Athens and became known as the Thirty. Xenophon records that "the people passed a resolution to choose thirty men who were to draft a constitution based on the ancestral laws of the State" and gives the full list of thirty names [11]. Grote likewise writes that a "Board of Thirty should be named, to draw up laws for the future government of the city" [10].

results.question: What did Maharbal say to Hannibal after the battle of Cannae?
results.ideal_answer: When Hannibal hesitated to march at once on Rome after Cannae, his cavalry commander Maharbal — who had urged that within five days Hannibal might dine in the Capitol — rebuked him: "You know how to conquer, Hannibal, but you do not know how to make use of your victory." Livy adds that this day's delay is believed to have saved the city and the empire.
results.answer: After the battle of Cannae, while others were congratulating Hannibal and urging him to rest, Maharbal, the commander of the cavalry, urged him to march on Rome immediately. He said, “Nay, rather, that you may know what has been achieved by this battle, five days hence you shall feast in triumph in the Capitol. Follow me: I will go first with the cavalry, that they may know that I am arrived before they know of me as approaching.” Hannibal replied that the proposal was too bold to decide on the spot and that he needed time to consider it. Maharbal then said, “Of a truth the gods have not bestowed all things upon the same person. You know how to conquer, Hannibal; but you do not know how to make use of your victory.” Livy adds that the delay of that single day is believed to have saved the city and the empire [1].
results.judge_notes: faithfulness: All claims and direct quotations in the answer are fully supported by cited passage [1] with no invented content. | completeness: The answer fully captures what Maharbal said, including his urging to march on Rome, the five-day boast, and the famous rebuke, thereby completely addressing the question. | attribution: The answer correctly attributes the information to the single relevant source (Livy) without any misattribution, and there are no disagreements to surface.

results.question: What trophy of gold rings was poured out before the Carthaginian senate to show the scale of the victory at Cannae?
results.ideal_answer: To prove the scale of the victory, Mago had the gold rings stripped from the Roman knights slain at Cannae poured out in the vestibule of the Carthaginian senate-house — a heap that, by report, filled about a peck (some claimed three and a half pecks) — explaining that only knights, and of these the principal only, wore such rings.
results.answer: According to Livy, after the Battle of Cannae, Hannibal's brother Mago reported to the Carthaginian senate and, as proof of the scale of the victory, ordered the golden rings stripped from fallen Roman knights to be poured out in the vestibule of the senate-house. The heap was so large that some claimed it measured three and a half pecks, though Livy notes the more credible account puts it at not more than one peck. The rings signified the slaughter of the Roman equestrian class, since only knights — and the chief among them — wore that ornament [1].
results.judge_notes: faithfulness: The answer accurately recounts the golden rings, their volume, and significance from passage [1], but embellishes by identifying the reporter as Hannibal's brother Mago, a detail not found in any provided source. | completeness: The answer fully covers what the question asks by identifying the trophy (gold rings stripped from fallen Roman knights), who presented it (Mago), where (the senate-house vestibule), the scale (peck measurements), and the significance (only knights wore them). | attribution: The answer correctly attributes the information to the single relevant source, Livy, without any misattribution or need for disagreement-surfacing.

results.question: What did Scipio Africanus say to the crowd on the anniversary of his victory when prosecuted, before leading them to the Capitol?
results.ideal_answer: Brought to trial on the very anniversary of the battle, Scipio reminded the assembly that on that day he had fought and beaten Hannibal and the Carthaginians in Africa (at Zama); declaring it unfit to spend such a day in litigation, he went up to the Capitol to thank the gods — and the whole assembly left the tribunes and followed him.
results.answer: On the anniversary of his victory over Hannibal at Zama, when Scipio Africanus was being prosecuted by the tribunes, he addressed the crowd from the rostrum, saying: "Tribunes of the people, and you, Romans, on the anniversary of this day I fought a pitched battle in Africa, with Hannibal and the Carthaginians, with good fortune and success. As, therefore, it is but decent that a stop be put, for this day, to litigation and wrangling, I will immediately go to the Capitol, there to return my acknowledgments to Jupiter the supremely good and great, to Juno, Minerva, and the other deities presiding over the Capitol and citadel, and will give them thanks for having, on this day, and at many other times, endowed me both with the will and ability to perform extraordinary services to the commonwealth. Such of you also, Romans, as it suits, come with me and beseech the gods that you may have commanders like myself; since, from my seventeenth year to old age, you have always anticipated my years with honours, and I, your honours with services." He then went from the rostrum to the Capitol, and the entire assembly followed him [2][4].
results.judge_notes: faithfulness: Every claim and quotation is directly supported by cited passages [2] and [4], with no invented or unsupported content. | completeness: The answer fully provides Scipio's speech, the context of being prosecuted by tribunes on the anniversary of Zama, and his leading the assembly to the Capitol, satisfying all parts of the question. | attribution: The sources agree on the details of Scipio's speech, and the answer accurately provides the direct quote with correct bare citation markers without any misattribution.

results.question: Which famous maxims of the sages were inscribed in the forecourt of the temple of Apollo at Delphi?
results.ideal_answer: The two famous maxims inscribed by the Seven Wise Men in the vestibule (forecourt) of the temple of Apollo at Delphi were "Know thyself" and "Nothing in excess" — the latter rendered by Pausanias as "Not too much of anything."
results.answer: According to Pausanias, the maxims inscribed in the vestibule (forecourt) of the temple of Apollo at Delphi were **"Know thyself"** and **"Not too much of anything"** (i.e., "Nothing in excess"). He records that these sayings were offered to Apollo by the Seven Wise Men [1].

results.question: What city did Alexander found in memory of the horse he had tamed as a boy?
results.ideal_answer: Bucephala, on the Hydaspes. As a boy Alexander tamed the horse Bucephalas (turning him toward the sun after noticing he feared his own shadow); when the horse died of age and toil after the battle with Porus, Alexander founded the city of Bucephala in his memory.
results.answer: Alexander founded the city **Bucephala** (also called Bucephalia) in memory of his horse Bucephalas, who died during the Indian campaign. Both Arrian and Plutarch record this. Arrian writes that Alexander founded two cities after the battle with Porus: "the one he named Nicaea... and the latter Bucephala in memory of his horse Bucephalas, which died there, not from having been wounded by any one, but from the effects of toil and old age" and that the city was "on the spot whence he started to cross the river Hydaspes" [1]. Plutarch similarly notes that Alexander "built a city, which he named Bucephalia, in memory of him, on the bank of the river Hydaspes" [2].

results.question: How did the wife of the emperor who added Britain to the empire meet her end?
results.ideal_answer: The emperor is Claudius, who invaded Britain to gain a triumph; his wife Messalina, after publicly marrying her lover Silius, was ordered to answer for her conduct, failed in her attempts at suicide in the gardens of Lucullus, and was run through with a sword by a tribune.
results.answer: Valeria Messalina, wife of the emperor Claudius (who annexed Britain), was put to death after her scandalous marriage to Gaius Silius was discovered. According to Suetonius, she retreated to the gardens of Lucullus, attempted suicide but lacked the resolve, and was killed when a tribune "burst into the gardens, and plunging his sword into her body" [2]. Tacitus adds that she "put her hand to a dagger" but failed to use it effectively, and "a blow from the tribune drove it through her" [4]; an earlier passage in Tacitus notes that Narcissus ordered the centurions and tribunes "to accomplish the deed of blood" [1]. Cassius Dio concisely records that after the plot was exposed, Claudius "slew Messalina" [3].

results.question: Who tutored the son of the king who crushed the Greeks at Chaeronea?
results.ideal_answer: Aristotle. The king is Philip of Macedon, victor at Chaeronea (where his son Alexander led the charge against the Theban Sacred Band); Philip had earlier sent for Aristotle, the most celebrated philosopher of the age, to instruct Alexander, rewarding him by rebuilding his native Stagira.
results.answer: The tutor was Aristotle. Philip II of Macedon, who crushed the Greeks at Chaeronea, sent for Aristotle to educate his son Alexander. Plutarch records that Philip 'sent for Aristotle, the most learned and most celebrated philosopher of his time' to instruct Alexander [4]. Grote notes that 'at the age of thirteen, Alexander was placed under the instruction of Aristotle' [2], and Bury similarly states that Philip called Aristotle 'to undertake the education of the crown prince' [1].
results.judge_notes: faithfulness: Every claim about Aristotle tutoring Alexander at Philip’s request is directly supported by the cited passages, and the reference to Chaeronea merely restates the question’s premise without introducing invented content. | completeness: The answer correctly and fully identifies Aristotle as the tutor of Alexander, son of Philip II of Macedon (the king who defeated the Greeks at Chaeronea), satisfying everything the question asks without requiring the reference's extra contextual details. | attribution: The sources agree on the answer, and the model correctly attributes the supporting details to the respective sources without any misattribution.

results.question: Who avenged the Roman commander killed at Carrhae?
results.ideal_answer: Ventidius (Publius Ventidius Bassus), Antony's lieutenant. The commander killed at Carrhae was Crassus, slain at a treacherous parley after the defeat by the Parthians; Ventidius later defeated the Parthians in three battles, killing prince Pacorus, a victory Plutarch says "fully avenged" the defeats under Crassus.
results.answer: Publius Ventidius (Publius Ventidius Bassus) avenged Crassus. According to Plutarch, Ventidius defeated and killed Pacorus — the Parthian king's son — in battle in Cyrrhestica, and this victory "fully avenged their defeats under Crassus" [16]. Cassius Dio likewise records that the Romans honoured Ventidius because "he had paid the Parthians in full through the death of Pacorus for the disasters that Roman arms had incurred in the time of Crassus" [17]. Ventidius, who had once marched as a captive in a Roman triumph, became the first Roman to celebrate a triumph over the Parthians [17][16].
results.judge_notes: faithfulness: All substantive claims—that Ventidius defeated and killed Pacorus in Cyrrhestica, that this victory avenged Crassus’s defeat at Carrhae, and that Ventidius was the first Roman to triumph over the Parthians—are directly and accurately supported by the cited Plutarch and Dio passages, with no invented narrative content. | completeness: The answer correctly identifies Publius Ventidius Bassus as the one who avenged Crassus and explains how he did so by defeating the Parthians and killing Pacorus, covering everything the question asks without omitting any required information. | attribution: The sources agree on the answer, and the model correctly attributes the details to Plutarch and Cassius Dio in prose without any misattribution.

results.question: Why did the Greek army Xenophon served with have to fight its way home?
results.ideal_answer: Their employer Cyrus the Younger was killed at Cunaxa charging his brother King Artaxerxes (struck by a javelin under the eye), leaving the Greeks victorious but stranded deep in Persia; then Tissaphernes treacherously seized their five generals at a conference and cut down the captains, leaving the Ten Thousand leaderless and forced to fight their way to the sea.
results.answer: The Greek army (the Ten Thousand) had to fight its way home because after Cyrus the Younger was killed at the Battle of Cunaxa, the Persians under Tissaphernes treacherously broke a truce. The Greeks had refused to surrender to King Artaxerxes after Cyrus's death [1]. Tissaphernes then negotiated an agreement to guide them safely back to Greece, but he lured their generals — Clearchus, Menon, Proxenus, Agias, and Socrates — into his tent and seized them, while his men killed the accompanying captains [4][7]. This betrayal left the Greek army stranded deep in hostile Persian territory, over a thousand miles from home, without leaders, without guides, without supplies, and hemmed in by impassable rivers and hostile tribes [7]. With no alternative, they were forced to fight their way north to the Black Sea on their own.
results.judge_notes: faithfulness: Every claim in the answer is supported by the provided passages, including the death of Cyrus, the refusal to surrender, Tissaphernes' agreement and subsequent betrayal, the seizure and killing of the generals and escort, and the army's stranded, leaderless condition deep in hostile territory. | completeness: The answer fully and correctly covers the reasons the Ten Thousand had to fight their way home: Cyrus the Younger’s death at Cunaxa left them stranded in Persia, and Tissaphernes’ treachery in seizing the generals and killing the captains left them leaderless and surrounded by enemies, forcing a fighting retreat. | attribution: The sources agree on the historical narrative, and the answer accurately synthesizes the information with correct citations and no misattributions.

results.question: How did Alexander react on finding the tomb of the founder of the Persian empire desecrated?
results.ideal_answer: Finding the tomb of Cyrus (whose epitaph read "I am Cyrus, son of Cambyses, who founded the empire of the Persians") dug through and pillaged at Pasargadae, Alexander was grieved; he commissioned Aristobulus to restore the tomb and its furnishings, seal the entrance, and he had the Magian guards arrested and tortured to find the culprits.
results.answer: Alexander was grieved by the desecration of Cyrus's tomb [3]. He commissioned Aristobulus to restore the tomb: to replace the body parts still preserved, repair the coffin, replace the funerary ornaments, and seal the entrance with the royal seal [2]. He arrested the Magian guards and put them to torture to discover the perpetrators, but they confessed nothing and were released [1]. According to Plutarch, Alexander also put to death a Macedonian named Polymachus, who was found to have committed the outrage [4].
results.judge_notes: faithfulness: All claims are directly supported by the provided source passages, including Alexander's grief, the commissioning of Aristobulus, the arrest and release of the Magians, and the execution of Polymachus. | completeness: The answer fully covers Alexander’s reaction—his grief, commissioning Aristobulus to restore and seal the tomb, and arresting and torturing the guards—without omitting any fact the question requires, and the extra details do not lower the score. | attribution: The sources provide complementary details about Alexander's reaction rather than disagreeing, and the answer correctly attributes each specific detail to its respective source without any misattribution.

results.question: Which Spartan commanded the Greeks at the land battle fought the same day as the sea battle at Mycale?
results.ideal_answer: Pausanias, son of Cleombrotos (regent for Leonidas' young son Pleistarchos). Herodotus reports that the victory at Plataea and the fight at Mycale fell on the same day, and that Pausanias was appointed to lead the Spartan army that won at Plataea.
results.answer: The Spartan who commanded the Greeks at the land battle fought the same day as the sea battle at Mycale was **Leotychides** (also spelled Leotychidas). He was the Spartan king and admiral of the Greek fleet that sailed to Mycale [12]. Herodotus describes him leading the Greeks at Mycale, making a proclamation to the Ionians before the battle, after which the Hellenes disembarked and engaged the Persians on land [11]. Pausanias likewise records that Leotychides, together with the Athenian general Xanthippus, took part in the action at Mycale [15]. The land battle was not a separate engagement with a different commander; it was the same action in which the Greek naval forces landed and fought the Persians ashore, with Leotychides in overall Spartan command.
results.judge_notes: faithfulness: All claims are fully supported by the provided passages, including Leotychides' role as Spartan king and admiral, his proclamation and disembarkation at Mycale, and his overall command of the unified land-and-sea action. | completeness: The answer incorrectly identifies Leotychides, who commanded at Mycale, instead of Pausanias, who led the separate land battle at Plataea that was fought the same day as Mycale. | attribution: The sources agree on the identity of the commander, and the answer correctly attributes the specific details to Herodotus and Pausanias in prose without any misattribution.

results.question: How did the statesman who championed Athens' Long Walls die?
results.ideal_answer: Pericles — who, as Socrates attested, proposed the long wall to the people — died of the plague of Athens, in his case not with violent fits but as a dull, lingering distemper that slowly wasted his body and mind.
results.answer: Pericles, the statesman who championed the construction of the third Long Wall connecting Athens to Piraeus [2], died from the plague that ravaged Athens during the Peloponnesian War. According to Plutarch, the plague seized him "not with sharp and violent fits, as it did others that had it, but with a dull and lingering distemper, attended with various changes and alterations, leisurely, by little and little, wasting the strength of his body, and undermining the noble faculties of his soul" [7]. Bury notes that after losing his two sons in the plague, Pericles "died about a year later" [10]. Grote's history also records his "last moments and death" following the epidemic and his temporary fall from favor [6].
results.judge_notes: faithfulness: Every claim is directly supported by the provided passages: Pericles championed the third Long Wall [2], died from the plague [7][10], and suffered a lingering illness before his death [6][7]. | completeness: The answer fully identifies Pericles and thoroughly explains that he died of the plague through a dull, lingering distemper that slowly wasted his body and mind, completely addressing what the question asks. | attribution: The sources agree on the cause of Pericles' death, and the answer correctly attributes the specific details and quotes to Plutarch, Bury, and Grote in prose without any misattribution.

results.question: How did the two leading assassins of Caesar die?
results.ideal_answer: Both died at Philippi. Cassius, mistakenly believing the battle lost, bared his neck to his freedman Pindarus and was beheaded; in the second battle Brutus, defeated, fell on his sword — held, by some accounts, by his friend Strato — and died.
results.answer:
The two leading assassins, **Cassius** and **Brutus**, both died by their own hands after the defeat at Philippi.

**Cassius:** After being driven from his fortifications and seeing his camp captured, he ascended a hill. Believing that his friend Titinius had been taken by the enemy, Cassius withdrew into a tent with his freedman Pindarus and bared his neck. He either ordered Pindarus to kill him, or Pindarus acted without orders; the head was found severed from the body. Cassius ended his life on his birthday, the same day the battle was fought [1][2].

**Brutus:** After his forces were defeated in the second battle, Brutus fled to a steep spot and despaired of safety. He asked his friend Strato, an Epirote, to help him die. According to Appian, Strato thrust his sword into Brutus’s side while Brutus did not flinch [4]. Plutarch reports that Brutus fell on his own sword, which Strato held for him, or that Brutus threw himself violently upon the blade while Strato turned aside his head [5]. Cassius Dio adds that before his death Brutus uttered a line from Heracles: “Unhappy Virtue, thou wert but a name…” and then called on one of the bystanders to kill him [9].
results.judge_notes: faithfulness: Grounded, but the claim that Brutus fell on his own sword while Strato held it is attributed to Plutarch [5] when that version appears in William Smith [7]; Plutarch [5] describes Brutus grasping the hilt himself. | completeness: The answer fully covers how both leading assassins died at Philippi, detailing Cassius’s beheading by Pindarus and Brutus’s assisted suicide with Strato, without omitting any part of what the question asks. | attribution: The answer correctly uses prose attribution to distinguish the varying accounts of Brutus's death from Appian, Plutarch, and Cassius Dio, while appropriately treating the compatible accounts of Cassius's death as agreement.

results.question: What revolt broke out in Britain under the emperor who was the last of his dynasty?
results.ideal_answer: The revolt of Boudicca (Buduica), queen of the Iceni, broke out under Nero — whom Dio calls the last of the descendants of Aeneas and Augustus. While the governor Paulinus was away at Mona she sacked two Roman cities with enormous slaughter before being defeated.
results.answer: The revolt that broke out in Britain was led by Boadicea (Boudica), queen of the Iceni, during the reign of the emperor Nero. Nero was the last emperor of the Claudian dynasty (the Julio-Claudian line) [10]. The revolt occurred in AD 61; Boadicea and the Britons rose against the Romans, defeated several Roman detachments, and attacked the colony at Camulodunum, before being ultimately defeated by the governor Suetonius Paulinus [9][10].
results.judge_notes: faithfulness: Every claim is fully supported by the cited passages, including Nero being the last of the Claudian dynasty, Boadicea's revolt in AD 61, and the defeat by Suetonius Paulinus. | completeness: The answer identifies the revolt of Boudicca, names Emperor Nero, and explicitly states he was the last of the Julio-Claudian dynasty, fully covering everything the question asks. | attribution: The sources agree on the details of the revolt, and the answer correctly attributes the information using bare citation markers without any misattribution.

results.question: How did the brother of Hannibal who marched an army into Italy to reinforce him meet his end?
results.ideal_answer: Hasdrubal, Hannibal's brother, crossed the Alps into Italy to reinforce him but was defeated and killed by the consuls Marcus Livius and Claudius Nero at the river Metaurus (207 BC); his severed head, carried south, was thrown before the outposts of Hannibal's camp.
results.answer: Hasdrubal Barca, Hannibal's brother, met his end at the Battle of the Metaurus in 207 BC. After his army was defeated by the combined forces of the consuls Claudius Nero and Marcus Livius, Hasdrubal, seeing the battle irretrievably lost, rushed into the midst of the enemy and fell fighting sword in hand, in a manner deemed worthy of his father Hamilcar and his brother Hannibal [9]. Appian simply records that he was slain along with most of his men [1], while Mommsen notes that he sought and found an honourable soldier's death like his father [3]. After the battle, the Romans cut off his head and threw it into Hannibal's camp as a grim announcement of the defeat [5][9].
results.judge_notes: faithfulness: The answer is mostly grounded, but it introduces unsupported embellishments such as the surname 'Barca' and the adjective 'grim' that do not appear in the provided passages. | completeness: The answer fully covers how Hasdrubal met his end, including his defeat at the Metaurus in 207 BC, his death in combat, and the Romans throwing his severed head into Hannibal's camp. | attribution: The sources agree on the core facts while providing varying levels of detail, and the answer correctly attributes the specific details to their respective sources in prose without any misattribution.

results.question: How did the Carthaginian commander who led elephants across the Alps eventually die, long after his war with Rome?
results.ideal_answer: Hannibal, who led his army with its elephants across the Alps into Italy in the Second Punic War, died decades later in exile: cornered at the court of king Prusias by a Roman demand for his surrender, he drank the poison he had long kept ready.
results.answer: Hannibal died by suicide, taking poison that he had long kept ready for such an event. After the Second Punic War, he eventually took refuge at the court of King Prusias in Bithynia. When the Roman envoy Titus Quinctius Flamininus demanded his surrender, Prusias sent guards to surround Hannibal's house. Finding all escape routes blocked, Hannibal drank poison, reportedly saying, "Let us release the Romans from their long anxiety" [1][4][2][3].
results.judge_notes: faithfulness: All substantive claims—Hannibal’s refuge with King Prusias, his house being surrounded by guards, and his suicide by poison with the quoted final words—are fully supported by the provided source passages. | completeness: The answer fully covers how Hannibal died by suicide with poison in exile at King Prusias's court after a Roman surrender demand, addressing all parts of the question. | attribution: The answer presents the method of Hannibal's suicide as settled fact, silently picking the poison account and ignoring the incompatible alternative methods (strangulation, drinking bull's blood) explicitly detailed in Plutarch.

results.question: Who commanded the Roman army that razed Carthage to the ground, and what did he reportedly weep over as the city burned?
results.ideal_answer: Scipio Aemilianus (Scipio the Younger), who destroyed Carthage in the Third Punic War (146 BC). As the city burned he shed tears and, asked by Polybius what he meant, admitted he frankly named his own country — fearing that one day the same fate would befall Rome.
results.answer: The Roman army that razed Carthage in 146 BC was commanded by **Scipio Aemilianus** (also called Scipio Africanus Minor). As the city burned, he reportedly wept. According to Appian, he shed tears and publicly lamented the fortune of the enemy, then after long meditation reflected on the rise and fall of cities, nations, and empires — including Troy, Assyria, Media, Persia, and Macedon — and when asked by his tutor Polybius what he meant, he replied that he feared for his own country, Rome, considering the mutability of human affairs [1]. Polybius's own account confirms that he stood by Scipio's side as Carthage burned and heard him utter with tearful eyes the foreboding of what might one day befall Rome [7]. William Smith's history adds that he is said to have repeated lines from Homer's *Iliad* over the flames: "The day shall come when sacred Troy shall perish, and Priam and his people shall be slain" [2].
results.judge_notes: faithfulness: Every substantive claim about Scipio’s command, his tears, the meditation on fallen empires, his reply to Polybius, and the Iliad quotation is directly supported by the source passages, with no invented narrative content. | completeness: The answer fully identifies Scipio Aemilianus and completely explains what he reportedly wept over—lamenting the enemy’s fortune and fearing Rome would one day share Carthage’s fate—while extra detail does not lower the score. | attribution: The sources agree and provide complementary details rather than disagreeing, and the answer correctly attributes each specific detail to its respective source in prose without any misattributions.

results.question: Which Theban general broke Spartan power in a great pitched battle, and where did he fall in his last victory?
results.ideal_answer: Epaminondas of Thebes, who shattered Spartan supremacy at Leuctra (371 BC) and fell mortally wounded at Mantinea (362 BC), dying in the very hour of his victory.
results.answer: The Theban general was Epaminondas. He shattered Spartan military dominance at the Battle of Leuctra (371 BCE) [2][1]. He fell in his last victory, the Battle of Mantinea (362 BCE), where he was mortally wounded while leading the decisive charge [4][5][7].
results.judge_notes: faithfulness: While the answer correctly identifies Epaminondas, Leuctra, and Mantinea from the sources, it invents the specific dates 371 BCE and 362 BCE, which appear in none of the provided passages. | completeness: The answer fully and correctly addresses both parts of the question by naming Epaminondas, identifying the Battle of Leuctra as where he broke Spartan power, and the Battle of Mantinea as where he fell in his last victory. | attribution: The sources agree on the facts regarding Epaminondas, the Battle of Leuctra, and his death at Mantinea, so the use of bare citation markers is correct and no prose attribution is required.

results.question: Which Achaean coastal city was swallowed by the sea in a single night, and what was blamed for the catastrophe?
results.ideal_answer: Helike (Helice) in Achaea, which an earthquake and an inundation of the sea overwhelmed in a single night (373 BC). The catastrophe was blamed on the wrath of Poseidon, whose suppliants the people of Helike had dragged from his temple and slain.
results.answer: The Achaean coastal city was Helice (also spelled Helike). It was destroyed by an earthquake and a subsequent tsunami that submerged it in a single night. The catastrophe was blamed on the anger of the god Poseidon (Neptune), because the people of Helice had refused to give the Ionians the sacred image of Poseidon or a model of his temple, despite a decree from the Achaean League ordering them to comply.
results.judge_notes: faithfulness: Every claim in the answer—including Helice's destruction, the nighttime earthquake and sea inundation, and the anger of Poseidon/Neptune over the refused image and temple model—is directly supported by the provided passages, particularly Strabo and Diodorus Siculus. | completeness: The answer names the city (Helice/Helike) and states that Poseidon's anger was blamed, fully answering both parts of the question. | attribution: The sources agree on the main points presented in the answer regarding the city, the events of the night, and the specific historical blame placed on Poseidon's anger, requiring no prose attribution or disagreement-surfacing.

results.question: How did the Spartan regent who led the Greeks to victory at Plataea fall from power and die?
results.ideal_answer: Pausanias the regent, who commanded the victorious Greeks at Plataea (479 BC), afterwards intrigued with Persia to make himself master of Greece; detected, he fled to the temple of Athena of the Brazen House, where the ephors roofed him in, barricaded the doors and starved him, bringing him out only as he was about to expire.
results.answer: Pausanias, the Spartan regent and victor of Plataea, fell from power through suspicion of treasonable dealings with Persia (medism) and a plot to stir up the Helots. Recalled to Sparta, he was initially imprisoned but released for lack of firm proof [3][2]. The ephors later obtained evidence through a letter entrusted to a servant; they overheard his confession in a staged meeting at the sanctuary of Taenarus [2][9]. When they moved to arrest him in the street, he fled for sanctuary to the temple of Athena Chalkioikos (the Brazen House). The ephors removed the roof of the small chamber where he had hidden, walled up the doors, and starved him until he was near death. They then brought him outside, where he died immediately, thus avoiding desecration of the temple [2][1][5]. Some accounts add that his own mother placed the first stone in the door [1]. Originally they intended to cast his body into the Kaiadas gorge, but he was buried nearby; later, at Delphi’s command, his tomb was moved to the place of his death and two bronze statues were dedicated to atone for the pollution [2][1].
results.judge_notes: faithfulness: Every claim in the answer is fully supported by the provided passages; the only inaccuracies are incorrect citation markers (e.g., the Taenarus letter and confession come from [9], not [2]), which per the instructions do not affect the faithfulness score. | completeness: The answer fully addresses both how Pausanias fell from power (suspicion of Persian collaboration, recall, imprisonment, and detection) and how he died (being walled up in the temple and starved), leaving no part of the question unanswered. | attribution: The sources agree on the core narrative of Pausanias's downfall and death, and the answer accurately synthesizes these details with correct citations, appropriately noting the extra detail about his mother as coming from 'some accounts'.

results.question: The empire whose supreme god was Asshur fell when its great capital was stormed — who destroyed that capital?
results.ideal_answer: Assyria — whose national god was Asshur and whose capital was Nineveh — fell when Nineveh was stormed by the Medes under Cyaxares allied with the Babylonians under Nabopolassar; the last Assyrian king, his means of resistance exhausted, burned himself in his palace.
results.answer: The Assyrian capital Nineveh was destroyed by a coalition of the Medes under Cyaxares and the Babylonians under Nabopolassar. Nabopolassar, an Assyrian general sent to defend Babylon, instead betrayed his king Saracus, allied with Cyaxares, and together they stormed Nineveh. Saracus, in despair, burned himself in his palace [3].
results.judge_notes: faithfulness: Every claim in the answer is directly supported by passage [3], including Nabopolassar's betrayal of Saracus, his alliance with Cyaxares, their joint effort against Nineveh, and Saracus burning himself in his palace, with no invented content. | completeness: The answer identifies the Assyrian capital as Nineveh and fully names the coalition of Medes under Cyaxares and Babylonians under Nabopolassar who destroyed it, thereby answering everything the question asks. | attribution: The answer correctly identifies the destroyers of Nineveh based on the single relevant source and accurately attributes the information without any misattribution or disagreement to resolve.

results.question: How did the founder of the Persian empire meet his death on campaign?
results.ideal_answer: Cyrus the Great, founder of the Persian empire, was killed on campaign beyond the Araxes against the Massagetae and their queen Tomyris; the greater part of his army was destroyed and Cyrus himself fell there, after a reign of twenty-nine years.
results.answer:
Cyrus the Great, founder of the Persian Empire, met his death on campaign against the Massagetae, a nomadic people ruled by Queen Tomyris on his north-eastern frontier. According to Herodotus, after an initial success in which he captured Tomyris's son Spargapises (who then killed himself), Cyrus fought a fierce pitched battle against the main Massagetae army. The Massagetae prevailed, the greater part of the Persian army was destroyed, and Cyrus himself was slain on the field [1]. Tomyris then had his corpse sought out among the dead and, in revenge for her son, plunged his head into a skin filled with human blood, saying she would give him his fill of blood as she had threatened [1]. Herodotus notes that many other tales exist about Cyrus's end, but he considers this one the most worthy of belief [5].

An alternative tradition, recorded by Ctesias and noted by the modern historian George Rawlinson, holds that Cyrus fell not against the Massagetae but against the Derbices, a people bordering India. In this version, Cyrus was mortally wounded in a battle in which the Derbices, aided by Indian allies with elephants, defeated him; though the Persians later won a complete victory with Sacaen reinforcements, Cyrus died of his wound on the third day [2]. Rawlinson concludes that while the exact circumstances are uncertain, it is tolerably well established that Cyrus died of a wound received in a campaign on his north-eastern frontier in 529 BC, after a reign of twenty-nine years, and that his body was conveyed back to Persia and buried at Pasargadae [2].
results.judge_notes: faithfulness: Every claim in the answer is directly supported by the provided source passages, including the details from Herodotus about the battle with the Massagetae and from Rawlinson/Ctesias about the alternative account involving the Derbices, with no invented content. | completeness: The answer fully describes how Cyrus the Great died on campaign, detailing the battle against the Massagetae, the destruction of his army, his death on the field, and even noting an alternative tradition, thereby covering everything the question asks without omitting any required element. | attribution: The answer correctly surfaces the disagreement among the sources regarding how Cyrus died and clearly attributes each distinct version to its respective source in prose.

results.question: Which Jewish prisoner predicted that his Roman captor would become emperor, and did the prophecy come true?
results.ideal_answer: Josephus, captured at Jotapata, predicted to Vespasian that he would become Caesar and emperor. The prophecy came true when the eastern legions proclaimed Vespasian emperor (AD 69) — Tiberius Alexander first swearing the legions of Egypt to him on 1 July — and Vespasian, recalling the prophecy, freed Josephus.
results.answer: The Jewish prisoner was Josephus (the historian). When he was captured by the Romans, he told Vespasian: "Thou, O Vespasian, art Caesar and emperor, thou, and this thy son" [2]. At first Vespasian did not believe him, but later, after Nero's death and the turmoil of AD 68–69, Vespasian did become emperor. Once in power, Vespasian recalled Josephus's prediction, acknowledged that "this man, who hath foretold my coming to the empire beforehand, and been the minister of a Divine message to me" should no longer be treated as a captive, and ordered his release [1]. So the prophecy indeed came true.
results.judge_notes: faithfulness: The answer is largely grounded in the cited passages, but it adds unsupported embellishment about 'the turmoil of AD 68–69' and specific dating not found in the provided sources. | completeness: The answer identifies Josephus as the prisoner and fully confirms the prophecy came true by describing Vespasian's rise to emperor and Josephus's subsequent release, covering everything the question asks. | attribution: The sources agree and complement each other, and the answer correctly attributes the specific details to the respective passages without any misattribution.

results.question: Which king rebuilt the Jerusalem temple on a grand scale, and how did he die?
results.ideal_answer: Herod the Great, who rebuilt the Temple at Jerusalem on a magnificent scale, died of a loathsome wasting disease — fever, ulcerated entrails, dropsy, and putrefying flesh that bred worms — shortly after putting his son Antipater to death.
results.answer: Herod the Great rebuilt the Jerusalem temple on a grand scale, undertaking the work in the eighteenth year of his reign and making it larger and more magnificent than before [3][1]. As for his death, he succumbed to a horrific disease: a slow internal fire, insatiable appetite, ulcerated entrails, intense colon pain, and dropsy (watery fluid around his feet) [20]. In his agony, he attempted suicide by asking for a knife to pare an apple, intending to stab himself, but was stopped by his cousin Achiabus [13]. He then ordered the execution of his son Antipater and died five days later [18].
results.judge_notes: faithfulness: All claims—including the rebuilding details, disease symptoms, attempted suicide, and the five-day interval before death—are directly supported by the provided source passages. | completeness: The answer fully covers both parts of the question, identifying Herod the Great and describing the horrific disease and circumstances of his death, without omitting anything explicitly asked. | attribution: The sources disagree on whether Herod began rebuilding the temple in the fifteenth or eighteenth year of his reign, but the answer silently picks the eighteenth year and presents the contested point as settled without surfacing the discrepancy.

results.question: How did the reign of the emperor Vitellius come to its end?
results.ideal_answer: Vitellius's army was crushed by the Flavian forces at the second battle of Cremona (Bedriacum); when Rome fell, Vitellius was dragged from hiding through the streets to the Gemonian stairs, tortured and killed, and his body cast into the Tiber.
results.answer:
Vitellius's reign ended in a violent downfall when the forces of Vespasian, led by Antonius Primus, invaded Italy and defeated his army. After realizing the war was lost, Vitellius attempted to abdicate publicly but was prevented by his own soldiers and supporters. As Vespasian's troops entered Rome, fierce fighting erupted in the city, during which the Capitoline Temple was burned [10][6].

Vitellius, fearing for his life, disguised himself in rags and hid in a dog kennel within the palace. He was soon discovered by enemy soldiers, who recognized him despite his disguise. Stripped, bound, and with a rope around his neck, the former emperor was dragged from the palace along the Via Sacra to the Forum, enduring relentless mockery, blows, and pelting with dung and mud [3][7][4]. His captors forced him to look up by pricking his chin with swords, and he was made to watch his statues being toppled. Tacitus and Cassius Dio record that in his abasement he uttered the famous words: “Yet I was your Emperor” [4][5].

Ultimately, Vitellius was taken to the Scalae Gemoniae (the Gemonian Stairs), where he was tortured and put to death with lingering blows. Some sources specify he was beheaded [2]; others say he was struck down by the soldiers [5]. After his death, his body was dragged with a hook and thrown into the Tiber. Josephus adds that over fifty thousand people were killed in the associated fighting [2]. His reign had lasted roughly eight months (or just under a year by Dio's reckoning) before this ignominious end [2][5].

results.question: After the catastrophe at Cannae, what feature of Rome's system of government was credited with explaining her swift recovery?
results.ideal_answer: Rome's mixed, balanced constitution — the three elements of consuls (monarchic), senate (aristocratic) and people (democratic), each checking the others — which Polybius describes precisely at the epoch of Cannae as the source of Roman stability and resilience.
results.answer: The Roman political constitution—specifically its mixed and balanced nature combining elements of monarchy, aristocracy, and democracy—was credited by the historian Polybius for Rome's swift recovery after the disaster at Cannae. He states that despite being 'indisputably beaten in the field,' the Romans 'by the peculiar excellence of their political constitution, and the prudence of their counsels, ... not only recovered their supremacy over Italy ... but before very long became masters of the whole world' [3]. Polybius also notes that the Romans achieved this constitution not through abstract reasoning but 'through many struggles and difficulties, and by continually adopting reforms from knowledge gained in disaster,' and he sets out to describe that constitution precisely at the period of Cannae because of its superiority [2].

results.question: Which oracle did Croesus famously test before his war with Persia, and what natural phenomenon there was later said to produce its prophecies?
results.ideal_answer: The oracle at Delphi, which Croesus tested (by the secret boiled-tortoise riddle) and judged the only true oracle before consulting it about war with Persia. Its prophecies were later attributed to an exhalation rising from a cleft in the earth, which inspired the Pythian priestess to her divine frenzy.
results.answer: Croesus tested the Oracle of Delphi (and several others) by sending messengers to inquire what he was doing on a specific day a hundred days after their departure. Only the Delphic oracle correctly perceived that he was boiling a tortoise and a lamb together in a bronze cauldron, which persuaded him that Delphi alone was true [1]. As for the natural phenomenon: the site was said to contain a chasm or opening in the earth from which a vapour or exhalation arose. This vapour was believed to inspire prophecy: Diodorus Siculus relates that goats and a shepherd were driven into a frenzy by the vapour, leading to the discovery of the oracle, and that the Pythia was later seated on a tripod over the chasm so she could be inspired by it without danger [11]. Pausanias similarly records that shepherds 'became inspired by the vapour' at Delphi [14].

results.question: Why is Roman concrete so durable compared to modern concrete?
results.answer: The ancient sources point to the special properties of the local volcanic sand (pozzolana) used by the Romans. Cassius Dio describes a volcanic earth near Baiae that, while crumbling in dry air, "when placed in a swirl of water and sand grow into a solid piece" and "as much of them as is in the liquid hardens and petrifies," becoming "indissoluble" [1]. Strabo, writing of the harbor at Puteoli, similarly notes that the sand there "contains much gypsum, and will cement and consolidate thoroughly" when mixed with chalk-stones (lime), enabling the construction of durable moles in the sea [10]. These descriptions highlight the very reasons modern researchers find Roman concrete superior to modern Portland cement: the volcanic ash-lime mixture can set and gain strength underwater, and it forms a material that is exceptionally resistant to cracking and chemical decay over centuries. The provided texts do not make an explicit comparison to modern concrete, but they record the ancient knowledge of the material's lasting, water-hardening quality.

results.question: What tactics did Hannibal use against Julius Caesar at the battle of Cannae?
results.answer: Hannibal did not face Julius Caesar at the battle of Cannae — the two were not contemporaries. Hannibal fought the Roman army at Cannae in 216 BC against the consuls Gaius Terentius Varro and Lucius Aemilius Paulus, over a century before Caesar's campaigns. The sources in the corpus describe Hannibal's tactics at Cannae in detail. Polybius reports that Hannibal arranged his infantry in a crescent (convex toward the enemy), placing Celtic and Iberian troops in the advanced centre with Libyan heavy-armed troops on the drawn-back wings. When the Roman legions pushed forward, the centre gave way, and the Libyans on both wings swung inward to attack the Roman flanks, enveloping them [4][6]. Plutarch describes the same crescent tactic, adding that Hannibal positioned his troops so that a violent wind blew dust into the Romans' faces, and he placed his best men in the wings so they could charge the Romans in the flank once they had pushed into the centre [2][8]. Cassius Dio notes that Hannibal ploughed the sandy ground beforehand to create a dust cloud and also planted ambuscades at the foot of the hills [1]. Appian similarly records that Hannibal chose ground where the east wind would be at his back and stationed cavalry and light-armed troops in ambush on a wooded hill to attack the enemy's rear once battle was joined [5]. The decisive element across all sources was Hannibal's superior cavalry, led by Hasdrubal, which routed the Roman horse on the wings and then charged the Roman infantry from the rear, completing the encirclement [7][8]. None of the sources connect these tactics to Julius Caesar in any way.

results.question: How did the emperor Augustus react when he was assassinated in the Senate?
results.answer: The question is based on a false premise: Augustus was never assassinated in the Senate. He died a natural death at Nola on August 19, AD 14, at the age of seventy-five (or seventy-six). According to Suetonius, on the day of his death he asked his friends, "Do ye think that I have acted my part on the stage of life well?" and then died suddenly in the kisses of Livia with the words "Livia! live mindful of our union; and now, farewell!" — a death he himself described as the easy euthanasia he had always wished for [21]. Cassius Dio similarly reports that Augustus fell sick and died at Nola, adding that Livia incurred some suspicion of poisoning him with figs but that the majority of trustworthy histories do not report foul play [1]. The image of a ruler assassinated in the Senate belongs to Julius Caesar, not Augustus — Caesar was stabbed twenty-three times by conspirators in the Senate-house on the Ides of March [8][10].

results.question: What deeds of Darius the Great are recorded on the Behistun inscription?
results.answer:
The Behistun Inscription records Darius the Great's suppression of a series of revolts that broke out across the Persian Empire early in his reign. According to Rawlinson, the inscription details the following deeds:

- A revolt in Susiana led by Atrines, who was captured and executed; a second Susianian revolt under Martes was quelled when the rebels killed their own leader upon hearing of Darius's approach [8].
- A revolt in Babylon led by a pretender claiming to be Nebuchadnezzar, son of Nabonidus. Darius defeated him in two battles—at the Tigris and at the Euphrates—took Babylon, and executed the pretender [8]. A second Babylonian revolt under Aracus (also claiming the name Nebuchadnezzar) was suppressed by the general Intaphres, and the rebel was crucified [14].
- A major rebellion in Media, Armenia, and Assyria under a Median pretender, Xathrites, who claimed descent from Cyaxares. After several battles fought by his generals, Darius took the field in person, defeated Xathrites at Kudrus, captured him, mutilated him (cutting off his nose, ears, and tongue), and eventually crucified him at Ecbatana [7][10].
- The extension of this rebellion into Parthia and Hyrcania, which were subdued with the help of Darius's father, Hystaspes [10].
- A revolt in Sagartia led by a native chief with similar pretensions; he was captured, mutilated, and crucified at Arbela [10].
- A revolt in Margiana under Phraates, quickly quelled by the satrap of Bactria [10].
- A revolt in Persia itself, where an impostor claimed to be Smerdis. He was defeated by Artabardes, captured, and crucified [14].
- A later revolt in Susiana suppressed by Gobryas, and another among the Sacae of the Tigris quelled by Darius in person, recorded in a supplementary portion of the inscription [2].

These campaigns, which occupied the first five or six years of Darius's reign, are the principal deeds commemorated on the Behistun rock.
results.judge_notes: refusal=False: The answer directly supplies a detailed list of Darius the Great's deeds as recorded on the Behistun Inscription, fully addressing the question asked.

results.question: What were the symptoms of the epidemic that devastated Athens?
results.ideal_answer: Thucydides (who had the disease himself) describes the "distemper" thus: people in good health were suddenly attacked by violent heats in the head, redness and inflammation of the eyes, a bloody throat and tongue with fetid breath, then sneezing, hoarseness, chest pain and a hard cough, stomach upset and retching with violent spasms. Most died around the seventh to ninth day; in survivors the disease could descend into the bowels (fatal diarrhoea), or cost them fingers, toes, eyes, or memory.
results.answer: According to Thucydides' eyewitness account, the epidemic that devastated Athens began suddenly in otherwise healthy individuals with violent heats in the head, and redness and inflammation of the eyes. The internal parts, including the throat and tongue, became bloody and emitted an unnatural, fetid breath [1]. These early symptoms were followed by sneezing and hoarseness, after which the pain descended to the chest, producing a hard cough. When it settled in the stomach, it caused upset and discharges of every type of bile, accompanied by great distress. Most cases also suffered ineffectual retching and violent spasms [1]. Externally, the body was reddish, livid, and broke out into small pustules and ulcers, though it was not very hot to the touch; internally, however, it burned so intensely that patients could not bear any clothing and craved cold water [2]. They were tormented by an inability to rest or sleep, and the body did not waste away while the disease was at its peak. Most died on the seventh or eighth day from internal inflammation. If they survived that stage and the disease descended into the bowels, violent ulceration and severe diarrhea brought on fatal weakness [2]. Even those who recovered were often left permanently marked: the disease could settle in the extremities — the privy parts, the fingers, and the toes — causing some to lose these parts, and others to lose their eyes. Some survivors also experienced a total loss of memory, failing to recognize themselves or their friends [2]. Additionally, birds and beasts that preyed on human bodies either avoided the corpses or died after tasting them, and domestic dogs showed the same effects [2][3].

results.question: What role did mass kidnapping play in early Rome's search for wives?
results.ideal_answer: Rome under Romulus was short of women and neighboring peoples refused intermarriage, so Romulus staged games (the Consualia) and invited the neighbors; at a signal the Roman youths rushed in and carried off the unmarried women — the "rape of the Sabine women." The ensuing war with the Sabines ended with the women themselves brokering peace and the two peoples merging.
results.answer: Mass kidnapping played a central role in early Rome's search for wives. According to Livy, Rome's population consisted largely of men, and when the Romans sought marriage alliances with neighboring peoples, they were rebuffed with the taunt, "Whether they had opened any asylum for women also, for that such a plan only could obtain them suitable matches?" [2]. Romulus then resorted to a stratagem: he proclaimed games in honor of Neptunus Equestris (the Consualia) and invited the neighboring Sabines, Latins, and others to attend [2][6]. At a prearranged signal during the festival, the Roman youth rushed upon the guests and carried off the unmarried daughters of the Sabines by force [2][4]. Plutarch adds that the seizure was specifically targeted at virgins — only one married woman, Hersilia, was taken, and that unknowingly — which Romulus used as his defense that the act was not wanton but designed to forge alliances through the "greatest and surest bonds" [4]. Romulus personally went among the captured women afterward, promising them lawful wedlock, shared property, and civil rights, and urging them to soften their anger [7]. The kidnapping thus served as the foundational solution to Rome's demographic crisis, securing wives for the settlers and ultimately leading, after war and the women's famous intervention, to the union of Romans and Sabines into one people [9][3].
results.judge_notes: faithfulness: Every claim in the answer—from Rome's lack of women and the rebuff by neighbors, to the Consualia stratagem, the targeted seizure of virgins, Romulus's promises, and the eventual unification—is directly grounded in the provided passages, with no invented content. | completeness: The answer fully explains the kidnapping's role as the foundational solution to Rome's wife shortage, covering the demographic context, the stratagem and execution, and the resulting marriages and union with the Sabines. | attribution: The sources agree on the historical narrative, and the answer accurately attributes the specific details to the correct authors in prose without any misattributions.

results.answer: According to the sources, the Sabine commander Mettius Curtius ended up in a swamp because his horse took fright or became mired during the battle. Livy gives a slightly different version: while fighting on horseback against Romulus and a band of Roman youths, Curtius was repulsed, and as the Romans pursued him, “his horse taking fright at the din of his pursuers, threw himself into a lake” [3]. In both accounts the place became known as the Curtian Lake.

results.question: Does Thucydides describe anything like a tsunami?
results.ideal_answer: Yes. He reports that during the earthquakes of 426 BC the sea at Orobiae in Euboea retired from the coast and returned in a huge wave, flooding part of the town and leaving some of it permanently under water, killing those who could not reach high ground (with a similar inundation at Atalanta). He even gives the correct causal explanation: the earthquake drives the sea back, and it recoils with redoubled force.
results.answer: Yes, Thucydides describes an event that closely resembles a tsunami. In Book 3 of his *History of the Peloponnesian War*, he recounts that during the sixth year of the war, after a series of earthquakes, the sea at Orobiae in Euboea suddenly retreated from the coastline and then returned as a huge wave that flooded much of the town, drowning those inhabitants who could not reach higher ground. He notes that part of the town was left permanently underwater. A similar inundation struck the island of Atalanta off the Opuntian Locrian coast, carrying away part of an Athenian fort and wrecking one of two ships drawn up on the beach. At Peparethus, the sea also retreated, though no inundation followed, and an earthquake threw down part of the town wall and other buildings. Thucydides himself attributes the phenomenon to earthquakes, explaining that where the shock is most violent, the sea is driven back and then recoils with redoubled force, causing the inundation [1].

results.question: Which Persian king was said to suffer from epilepsy from birth?
results.ideal_answer: Cambyses. Herodotus reports that he was said to have suffered from birth from a grievous malady "called by some the sacred disease" (the ancient name for epilepsy), and remarks it was no surprise that with so diseased a body his mind was not sound either.
results.answer: According to Herodotus, the Persian king Cambyses was said to have suffered from epilepsy from birth. Herodotus writes that Cambyses had from his birth a certain grievous malady, "that which is called by some the 'sacred' disease" [6] — the "sacred disease" being the ancient Greek term for epilepsy.

results.question: How were the pontoon bridges across the Hellespont built?
results.ideal_answer: After a storm destroyed the first pair (built by Phoenicians with flax cables and Egyptians with papyrus cables), the rebuilt bridges rested on rows of anchored fifty-oared galleys and triremes — 360 under one bridge and 314 under the other — over which cables of white flax and papyrus were stretched tight with wooden windlasses; planks, brushwood, and stamped earth were laid on top, with barriers so the animals would not see the sea.
results.answer: The pontoon bridges across the Hellespont were built by lashing together two lines of ships—triremes and penteconters—moored across the strait. The bridge nearer the Euxine (Black Sea) used 360 vessels, and the other used 314 [7][9]. The ships were anchored at both prow and stern with very large anchors to hold them against the winds and currents [7]. Across each line of ships, six enormous cables were stretched—two of white flax and four of papyrus—and tightened from the shore using wooden windlasses [7][5]. On top of these taut cables, planks of wood were sawn to the width of the bridge and laid in order, then fastened down with a second layer of cables above them [7]. Over this foundation, brushwood was spread, then earth was carried on and stamped down firmly to form a solid causeway. Finally, a high palisade (barrier) was built along each side so that the baggage-animals and horses would not be frightened by seeing the water [7][2]. Three gaps were left between the ships and under the cables to allow small trading vessels to pass to and from the Euxine [9][7]. The first attempt at the bridges was destroyed by a storm, after which Xerxes had the engineers beheaded and the Hellespont itself scourged; the work was then entrusted to new engineers who built the more solid structure described above [7][2].
