results.question: What solar eclipse marked the departure of Xerxes' army, and how was it interpreted?
results.ideal_answer: As the army set out from Sardis in spring, the sun left its place in a perfectly clear sky and day became night. Xerxes, concerned, asked the Magians, who declared the god was foreshowing to the Greeks the abandonment of their cities — since the sun was the foreshower of events for the Greeks, the moon for the Persians. Xerxes marched on greatly pleased.
results.answer: According to Herodotus, as Xerxes' army set forth from Sardis at the beginning of spring to march to Abydos, a solar eclipse occurred: "the Sun left his place in the heaven and was invisible, though there was no gathering of clouds and the sky was perfectly clear; and instead of day it became night" [4]. Xerxes was concerned and asked the Magians what it portended. They declared that the god was foreshowing to the Hellenes a leaving of their cities, explaining that the Sun was the foreshower of events for the Hellenes, but the Moon for the Persians [4]. Reassured by this favorable interpretation, Xerxes proceeded on the march with very great joy [4]. (The historian J. B. Bury notes that this eclipse actually occurred two years later, and that the tradition Herodotus followed transposed its date to a more impressive and significant occasion [2].)
results.judge_notes: faithfulness: Every claim is directly supported by the source passages, including the eclipse description, the Magians' interpretation, Xerxes' reaction, and Bury's note that the eclipse actually occurred two years later. | completeness: The answer fully covers both parts of the question by identifying the solar eclipse at Sardis and detailing the Magians' interpretation that it foretold the Greeks abandoning their cities, and extra scholarly context does not reduce the completeness score. | attribution: The answer correctly and clearly attributes the historical account of the eclipse and its interpretation to Herodotus, while explicitly noting J.B. Bury's modern historical correction in prose.

results.question: How did the Persian royal postal relay system work?
results.ideal_answer: According to Herodotus, men and horses were stationed along the route, one for each day's journey; the message was carried by the first rider to the second, the second to the third, and so on, like the Greek torch-race. Neither snow, rain, heat, nor darkness of night stopped them — nothing mortal travelled faster. The Persians called it the angareion.
results.answer: The Persian royal postal relay system worked by a series of post-houses placed at regular intervals along the major routes. At each station, couriers and fresh relays of horses were maintained at state expense. A courier would receive the dispatch, ride at full speed to the next station, and hand it to another courier mounted on a fresh horse, who would then carry it further. This process continued from station to station, day and night, so that the message traveled continuously without delay. The couriers raced like torch-bearers in a Greek torch-race, and the Persians called this express horse-relay system "angareion" [3][1].
results.judge_notes: faithfulness: Every detail in the answer—from the post-houses, state-funded couriers and horses, relay handoffs, and day-and-night travel to the torch-race simile and the term angareion—is directly grounded in source passages [1] and [3]. | completeness: The answer fully explains the relay mechanism, station handoffs, fresh horses, continuous day-and-night operation, the torch-race analogy, and the system's name, completely addressing how the postal system worked. | attribution: The sources agree and provide complementary details about the postal system, and the answer accurately synthesizes this information without misattribution or any need to surface disagreements.

results.question: Which people beyond Scythia practiced cannibalism?
results.ideal_answer: The Androphagoi ("man-eaters"). Herodotus describes them as nomads dwelling north of Scythia with their own language and the most savage manners of all human beings, acknowledging no law — and alone of all these nations they are man-eaters.
results.answer: According to Herodotus, the Androphagoi (Androphagi) were a people who lived beyond Scythia and were man-eaters, described as the only ones among the nations in that region to practice cannibalism [1]. The Massagetae, who lived east of the Caspian Sea beyond Scythia, also practiced a form of cannibalism: when a person became very old, their relatives would sacrifice them along with cattle, then boil and feast upon the flesh [12][2]. This was considered the happiest fate, while dying of disease was seen as a misfortune because they would not be eaten.

results.question: What celibate Jewish brotherhood that held all property in common and despised private wealth is described at length?
results.ideal_answer: The Essenes. Josephus devotes long passages to this Jewish sect: they reject pleasure, neglect marriage, hold riches in contempt, and own nothing privately — by their law all property is thrown into a common stock, "so that a rich man enjoys no more of his own wealth than he who hath nothing at all," with about four thousand living this way.
results.answer: The brotherhood is the Essenes (also spelled Essens). Josephus describes them at length: they are a Jewish sect that rejects pleasures, esteems continence, neglects wedlock, and holds all property in common, so that no one has more than another and riches are despised [3]. Another passage notes they neither marry wives nor keep servants, and have all things in common, with about four thousand men living this way [1].

results.question: What stepped, multi-tiered temple-tower did the Babylonians raise at the center of their cities?
results.ideal_answer: The ziggurat — a solid temple-tower built in receding stages (square set upon square), the most essential feature of a Babylonian temple, with a shrine at its summit. The number of stages ranged from two up to seven or eight; the great temple of Belus at Babylon was the famous example.
results.answer: The Babylonians raised a ziggurat, a stepped tower built in stages, at the center of their cities [1].

results.question: What surprise attack on the beached Athenian fleet brought the long war between Athens and Sparta to an end?
results.ideal_answer: The battle of Aegospotami (405 BC). With the Athenian crews scattered ashore in search of food, Lysander rowed across the Hellespont and caught the fleet drawn up on the open beach; all but a handful of ships (Conon escaped with nine) were taken without a fight. The loss of the fleet left Athens unable to continue the Peloponnesian War, and she surrendered the next year.
results.answer: The surprise attack was the **Battle of Aegospotami** (405 BC). The Spartan commander Lysander, after four days of refusing battle and observing the Athenians' routine, launched a sudden assault on the fifth day. As soon as the Athenian crews had disembarked and scattered along the shore for their meal, a signal was given and Lysander's entire fleet rowed swiftly across the Hellespont from Lampsacus [1][4]. The Athenian fleet of 180 ships was caught almost entirely unmanned and defenceless at its moorings on the open beach; only a handful of vessels under Conon managed to escape [1][2]. The near-total destruction of Athens' last fleet left the city unable to continue the war, bringing the long Peloponnesian War to its decisive end.
results.judge_notes: faithfulness: The battle narrative is fully grounded in the cited passages, but the answer introduces the specific year 405 BC and the name 'Peloponnesian War,' neither of which appear in the provided sources, constituting minor unsupported embellishment. | completeness: The answer fully identifies the Battle of Aegospotami, describes the surprise assault on the beached and unmanned Athenian fleet, and explains how it decisively ended the Peloponnesian War, covering everything the question asks. | attribution: The sources agree on the details of the surprise attack at Aegospotami, and the answer accurately synthesizes this information with correct citation markers without any misattribution.

results.question: How were the victorious Athenian commanders punished for failing to rescue survivors after the sea battle at Arginusae?
results.ideal_answer: After winning at Arginusae (406 BC) the generals were accused of failing to pick up the shipwrecked crews (they blamed a storm). The Athenian assembly, illegally trying them collectively rather than one by one, condemned the eight; the six who had returned to Athens were executed. The city soon repented of the act.
results.answer: After the battle of Arginusae, the eight Athenian generals who had been present were tried by the Assembly (not the ordinary law-courts) on the charge of failing to rescue the crews of the disabled ships [1]. They were all condemned to death and their property confiscated in a single collective vote — a proceeding that violated the recognised usage of judging each defendant separately [1]. Six of the generals, including Thrasyllus and Pericles (the son of the great statesman), were executed; the other two kept away and escaped [1]. Socrates, who happened to be one of the prytanes that day, alone refused to put the illegal motion to the vote [1].
results.judge_notes: faithfulness: All claims are grounded in the provided passages, but the detail that Socrates alone refused to put the question to the vote is found in [3] and [5], not in the cited [1]. | completeness: The answer fully covers how the commanders were punished by describing their trial before the Assembly, the illegal collective vote, their condemnation to death, confiscation of property, and the execution of six generals, omitting no part of what the question asked. | attribution: The passages agree on the punishment of the Athenian commanders, and the answer correctly uses bare citation markers to attribute the synthesized details without any prose misattribution.

results.question: How was a Roman army's overnight fortified camp laid out, with its regular streets and gates?
results.ideal_answer: On a fixed, repeatable plan. The general's tent (praetorium) was set first, then the lines of the tribunes' and legions' quarters measured off from it; the whole camp formed a square crossed by regular streets (viae) "like a town", with four gates, a market-place, and a broad clear space inside the rampart — so familiar that each soldier could find his quarters as in his own city.
results.answer:
A Roman army's overnight fortified camp was laid out as a regular square, planned like a town with straight streets and four gates [3][4]. The process began by selecting a site and marking the general's tent (Praetorium) in the centre, from which all other measurements were taken [10][9].

**Streets (viae):** The camp was divided by a grid of broad, straight roads. A main transverse road, the *Via Principalis* (100 feet wide), ran across the camp in front of the tribunes' tents and the Praetorium [1][10]. Parallel to this, further forward, ran the *Via Quintana* (50 feet wide), so called because it passed the fifth maniples and squadrons [2][6]. Additional roads ran between the blocks of tents, forming a system of cross-roads, all of regular width [8]. A 200-foot empty space was left between the tents and the rampart on every side, which served for troop movements and other uses [3].

**Gates:** The camp had four gates, one on each side. The front gate was the *Porta Praetoria*, and the rear gate the *Porta Decumana* [6]. The two side gates were the *Porta Principalis Dextra* (right) and *Porta Principalis Sinistra* (left) [1]. Josephus confirms that the Romans "erect four gates, one at every side of the circumference" [4].

**Defences:** The camp was surrounded by a rampart (*agger*) and a ditch (*fossa*), constructed by the soldiers themselves [7]. The rampart was typically armed with palisades, and the ditch was of equal depth and breadth [5].

**Internal arrangement:** The interior was divided into regular blocks for the cavalry and infantry of the legions and allies, with the tribunes' tents in a line behind the Praetorium, and the quaestorium and marketplace nearby [10][8][2][3]. Every soldier knew exactly where his tent would be, so entering camp was "exactly like a legion entering its own city" [9].
results.judge_notes: faithfulness: The answer is largely grounded but includes unsupported embellishments, specifically naming the front gate 'Porta Praetoria' and stating the tribunes' tents were behind the Praetorium, neither of which is found in the provided passages. | completeness: The answer fully covers how the camp was laid out from the central praetorium, describes the regular streets (viae) including their names and grid pattern, and details all four gates, directly addressing every element the question asks for. | attribution: The sources agree and complement each other without any contradictions, and the answer correctly attributes the synthesized details to the appropriate passages using citation markers.

results.question: What strategy of refusing open battle and wearing the invader down by delay was used against Hannibal in Italy?
results.ideal_answer: The cautious, delaying strategy of the dictator Quintus Fabius Maximus, who shadowed Hannibal from the heights, cut off his supplies and refused a pitched battle — earning the surname Cunctator ("the Delayer"/"Lingerer") and, at first, scorn at Rome, before it was recognised as the policy that saved the state.
results.answer:
The strategy of refusing open battle and wearing down the invader by delay was employed by the Roman dictator **Quintus Fabius Maximus**, who was given the nickname **Cunctator** ("the Delayer") for this very policy. After the disastrous Roman defeats at the Trebia and Lake Trasimene, Fabius took command and deliberately avoided pitched battle with Hannibal. Instead, he kept his army on high ground where the Carthaginian cavalry could not reach him, shadowed Hannibal's movements at a safe distance, prevented his men from foraging or scattering, and relied on Rome's superior reserves of men and supplies to gradually exhaust the enemy [1][10][3][2].

Plutarch writes that Fabius aimed at "wearing out and wasting the vigor of his arms by lapse of time" and that Hannibal alone recognized the danger of this tactic, seeing that "the Carthaginians, unable to use the arms in which they were superior, and suffering the continual drain of lives and treasure in which they were inferior, would in the end come to nothing" [1]. Polybius explains that Fabius "fell back upon those resources in which the Romans had the advantage of the enemy... an inexhaustible supply of provisions and of men" and that his object was "to gradually diminish the limited numbers of the enemy" while avoiding a set battle [3]. Livy notes that Fabius kept his troops on the high ground, "so as neither to let him go altogether nor yet to encounter him" [2]. Cassius Dio records that Fabius was even given the name "The Delayer" (Cunctator) by his fellow-citizens for this approach [5]. Appian likewise confirms that Fabius "followed Hannibal closely, but did not come to an engagement with him, although often challenged" [6].
results.judge_notes: faithfulness: The answer is largely grounded in the passages but includes unsupported embellishment by naming specific prior battles (Trebia and Lake Trasimene) that do not appear in the provided sources. | completeness: The answer fully identifies Quintus Fabius Maximus and his delaying strategy (Cunctator), clearly explaining the refusal of pitched battle and the tactics used to wear Hannibal down in Italy. | attribution: The sources agree on the details of Fabius's strategy, and the answer correctly attributes the specific contributions and quotes to their respective authors in prose without any misattribution.

results.question: How did Hannibal's men break apart the rock blocking a pass while crossing the Alps?
results.ideal_answer: They split it by fire and vinegar: piling and burning timber against the rock to heat it, then pouring vinegar on the heated stone to make it soft and crumbling, after which they cut a way through with iron tools.
results.answer: Hannibal's men broke apart the rock blocking the pass by using fire and vinegar. According to Livy, they felled large trees, built a huge pile of timber, and when a strong wind arose, set fire to it. They then poured vinegar on the heated stones, which rendered them soft and crumbling, and opened a way through the rock with iron instruments [1]. Appian gives a similar account: they cut down and burned wood, quenched the ashes with water and vinegar, making the rocks brittle, and then shattered them with iron hammers [10]. Polybius describes the construction of a road on the face of the precipice but does not mention the fire-and-vinegar technique [6].
results.judge_notes: faithfulness: Every claim in the answer is directly supported by the cited passages, with no invented content or misattribution. | completeness: The answer fully explains the fire-and-vinegar method (heating the rock with burning timber, then pouring vinegar to soften it, and finally breaking through with iron tools) that the question asks for, and extra source details do not lower the score. | attribution: The sources agree on the method used (with Polybius merely omitting the specific fire-and-vinegar details), and the answer correctly attributes the distinct accounts to Livy, Appian, and Polybius in prose without any misattribution.

results.question: What secret nocturnal religious society did the Roman senate outlaw as a criminal conspiracy?
results.ideal_answer: The Bacchanalia — the secret night-rites of Bacchus. After they spread from Etruria through Italy and into Rome, the senate in 186 BC treated them as a dangerous conspiracy: thousands were tried and many executed, the meeting places demolished, and a decree forbade the rites in Rome or Italy except under strict, tiny, senate-licensed exceptions.
results.answer: The secret nocturnal religious society that the Roman senate outlawed as a criminal conspiracy was the **Bacchanalians** — the followers of the Bacchanalian rites (the worship of Bacchus). According to Livy, the senate, alarmed by reports of "conspiracies and nightly meetings" that might produce "secret treachery and mischief," committed to the consuls an extraordinary inquiry "concerning the Bacchanals and their nocturnal orgies" and decreed that "no persons initiated in the Bacchanalian rites should presume to come together or assemble on account of those rites" [1]. The senate subsequently passed a further decree "that no Bacchanalian rites should be celebrated in Rome or in Italy" [2]. Mommsen likewise describes it as "a secret nocturnal festival in honour of the god Bacchus" that spread like a cancer, corrupting families and giving rise to heinous crimes, for which more than 7,000 were punished [9].
results.judge_notes: faithfulness: Every claim is directly supported by the cited passages, with no invented content or unsupported embellishment. | completeness: The answer correctly identifies the Bacchanalians/Bacchanalia and fully covers every element the question asks for—secret, nocturnal, religious society outlawed by the Roman senate as a criminal conspiracy—without omitting any required detail. | attribution: The sources agree on the identity of the society, and the model correctly attributes the supporting details to Livy and Mommsen in prose without any misattribution.

results.question: What intoxicating vapor rising from a cleft in the ground was believed to put the priestess at Delphi into a prophetic frenzy?
results.ideal_answer: An exhalation rising from a deep cleft or cavern at Delphi. The Pythian priestess sat on a tripod set over the opening, breathed in the rising exhalation, and, seized by a divine frenzy, delivered the oracle's response.
results.answer: The intoxicating vapor that rose from the cleft at Delphi and was believed to induce the Pythia's prophetic frenzy was called **pneuma** (the sacred breath). A footnote in Samuel Dill's *Roman Society from Nero to Marcus Aurelius* quotes Cassius Dio referring to it as "τὸ ἱερὸν πνεῦμα" (the sacred breath) that issued from the chasm [9]. Other sources describe it simply as a "vapour" [6] or "a certain gas or vapor exhaling from the rock" [12], but the specific term used in antiquity was *pneuma*.

results.question: What Greek coastal city was swallowed by the sea after an earthquake, leaving its ruins visible beneath the water?
results.ideal_answer: Helike, in Achaea. One winter night (373 BC) a violent earthquake struck and the sea simultaneously rushed in, submerging the city and its people; for a long time afterwards its ruins could be seen under the water, growing fainter as the sea wore them away.
results.answer: The Greek coastal city was **Helice** (also spelled Helike), located in the region of Achaia on the northern coast of the Peloponnese. According to the sources, it was destroyed and submerged by the sea following a devastating earthquake. Strabo reports that Helice "was overwhelmed by the waves two years before the battle of Leuctra" and that the inundation occurred during the night, with the city originally standing 12 stadia from the sea [4]. Pausanias describes how "the sea encroached over much of the district and quite flooded Helice with water" and notes that "visible too are the ruins of Helice, but not quite as clearly as formerly, because they have been effaced by the action of the sea" [3]. Diodorus Siculus adds that the earthquake happened at night, and when survivors emerged at daybreak, the sea "raged to that degree, and broke in with that violence, that it swallowed up them and their houses together" [2]. Strabo also mentions the nearby city of Bura, which was destroyed in the same earthquake, but Helice was the one covered by the waves [1].

results.question: What great temple on the Capitol was burned down during the street fighting in Rome in the civil war of AD 69?
results.ideal_answer: The temple of Jupiter Optimus Maximus (the Capitol) was burned to the ground during the fighting between the Vitellians and the supporters of Vespasian. Tacitus calls it the most deplorable disgrace to befall Rome since the city's foundation — the seat of Jupiter, untouched by Porsenna or the Gauls, openly besieged and set on fire.
results.answer: The great temple on the Capitol that was burned down during the street fighting in Rome in AD 69 was the Temple of Jupiter Optimus Maximus (Jupiter Best and Greatest). Tacitus describes it as "the seat of Jupiter Supremely Good and Great" and laments that it "was burnt to the ground" during the conflict between the Vitellianists and the supporters of Vespasian [1][2]. Cassius Dio similarly records that the soldiers of Vitellius "burned down with other structures the great temple" [4], and Josephus notes that the soldiers "plundered the temple of its ornaments, and set it on fire" [7].
results.judge_notes: faithfulness: The answer correctly identifies the temple as the seat of Jupiter Supremely Good and Great (i.e., Jupiter Optimus Maximus) and accurately grounds every claim about its destruction in the provided passages. | completeness: The answer correctly identifies the Temple of Jupiter Optimus Maximus and directly addresses the question without omitting any required information. | attribution: The sources agree on the events, and the answer correctly attributes the specific details and quotes to their respective authors in prose without any misattribution.

results.question: How did the soldiers turn against the emperor Galba and kill him in the forum?
results.ideal_answer: Bought over by Otho's promises, the praetorians abandoned Galba; as he was carried into the Forum his bearers panicked and tipped him from his litter near the Lacus Curtius, where the soldiers cut him down — most reports saying a legionary named Camurius severed his throat — and then beheaded and mutilated the old emperor, consul and high priest, before the senators and crowd.
results.answer:
The soldiers turned against Galba primarily through the machinations of Marcus Salvius Otho, who had expected to be adopted as Galba's successor and was enraged when Piso was chosen instead. Otho won over the Praetorian Guard through bribery and promises, exploiting existing resentment among the troops who had not received an expected donative from the parsimonious Galba [3][6][11].

On the day of the murder (15 January 69 AD), Otho slipped away from Galba's side during a sacrifice, feigning an errand, and was taken to the Praetorian camp where the soldiers hailed him as emperor [8][9]. When Galba learned of the coup, he was initially deceived by false reports that the mutiny had been quelled and that Otho had been killed. A soldier even approached him brandishing a bloody sword, claiming to have slain Otho, which Galba questioned with "By what authority?" [3][1].

Emboldened by these false assurances, Galba set out in a litter toward the Forum to offer sacrifice at the Capitol. As he reached the middle of the Roman Forum, the tide turned: horsemen and foot-soldiers loyal to Otho met him. The decisive moment of defection came when the standard-bearer of Galba's own escort cohort, one Atilius Vergilio, tore off and dashed to the ground Galba's effigy — a signal that the troops had declared for Otho [4].

The soldiers then rushed into the Forum "in all the terror of their arms, and at the full speed of their horses," thrusting aside the crowd and trampling on the Senate [2]. Near the Lacus Curtius, Galba's litter-bearers panicked and threw him to the ground. He was then cut down by the soldiers in the presence of many senators and crowds of plebeians [1]. His last words are variously reported: some say he asked what wrong he had done and begged a few days to pay the donative; others that he voluntarily offered his neck to the murderers, bidding them strike if it was for the good of the Commonwealth [4].

After killing him, the soldiers foully mutilated his body, cutting off his head and parading it on a lance to Otho [1][7]. The centurion Sempronius Densus alone defended Galba and was slain alongside him [1]. Piso, Galba's adopted heir, was also hunted down and killed at the temple of Vesta [7].

results.question: On what furniture did well-to-do Romans recline while eating the main meal of the day?
results.ideal_answer: They reclined on couches (lecti) rather than sitting. The cena (dinner) was the chief meal of the day, and after the Punic wars special dining rooms — the triclinia, named for the three couches arranged around the table — were built for the comfort of reclining guests, with no more than three to a couch as good manners.
results.answer: Well-to-do Romans reclined on dining couches known as **lecti** (singular *lectus*) while eating the main meal of the day, the *cena*. These couches were arranged in a special dining room called a **triclinium** [2][1]. Typically, three couches were placed around a table, each accommodating three guests, making a total of nine—a number considered both sacred and convenient [1]. The host and the most important guest occupied specific positions: the guest of honour reclined on the *lectus medius* (middle couch) in the place called the *lectus consularis*, while the host reclined on the couch below him [1]. Earlier Roman custom also involved the head of the household reclining on a *lectus* while his wife sat beside him and children on stools, though this simpler arrangement later gave way to the elaborate *triclinia* [4].
results.judge_notes: faithfulness: Every claim in the answer—from the use of lecti/triclinia, the seating arrangement for host and guest, the number nine, and the older household custom—is directly supported by the provided passages, with no invented content. | completeness: The answer directly and fully identifies the furniture as couches (lecti) while addressing the main meal, satisfying the question's requirements without omitting anything asked. | attribution: The sources agree as they are all excerpts from the same book, and the answer correctly supports its claims with accurate bare citation markers without any misattribution.

results.question: How does Plutarch portray Antony's ruin through Cleopatra?
results.ideal_answer: Plutarch frames Cleopatra as "the last and crowning mischief" that befell Antony — a love that awakened his dormant passions and corrupted what goodness and judgment remained. She kept him captive through ceaseless charm (dicing, drinking, hunting with him, never letting him escape by day or night); the passion rekindled "like Plato's restive horse" when he returned east and showered her with provinces; and after Actium it left him a misanthrope playing Timon of Athens in a sea-hut, before the final catastrophe in Alexandria.
results.answer:
Plutarch portrays Antony's ruin through Cleopatra as a progressive moral and political collapse driven by infatuation. He sets up the theme explicitly, calling Cleopatra "the last and crowning mischief that could befall him" — a passion that awakened and kindled Antony's dormant furies and "stifle[d] and finally corrupt[ed] any elements that yet made resistance in him, of goodness and a sound judgment" [1].

Plutarch traces this ruin through several stages:

**Ensnarement and abdication of duty.** Cleopatra's calculated charm — her beauty, voice, and command of many languages — so captivated Antony that while his wife Fulvia fought his political battles in Rome and the Parthians massed in Mesopotamia, he allowed himself to be "carried away by her to Alexandria, there to keep holiday, like a boy, in play and diversion, squandering... time" [8]. Even after marrying Octavia, the "mischief" of his passion for Cleopatra rekindled "upon his approach to Syria" — Plutarch likens it to "Plato's restive and rebellious horse of the human soul, flinging off all good and wholesome counsel, and breaking fairly loose" [5].

**Humiliating subservience.** Plutarch catalogues Antony's public degradation at Cleopatra's hands: he gave her the library of Pergamus; at a banquet before many guests he rose and rubbed her feet; he allowed the Ephesians to salute her as queen; he read her amorous messages openly on the tribunal; and when she passed by in her chair he "started up and left [a Roman pleader] in the middle of their cause, to follow at her side and attend her home" [4]. Caesar later declared that Antony had been reduced to letting "a woman exercise [authority] in his place," his generals being her eunuch and hairdressing-girls [6].

**Alienation of allies.** Cleopatra's arrogance drove away Antony's most important supporters. Her ill-treatment of Titius and Plancus — "men of consular dignity and friends to Antony" — caused them to defect to Caesar and reveal the contents of Antony's will, which ordered his body sent to Cleopatra at Alexandria even if he died in Rome [10]. Other friends, including Marcus Silanus and the historian Dellius, were "driven from him by the insolent usage they had from Cleopatra's flatterers" [4].

**The fatal flight at Actium.** The decisive moment comes when Cleopatra's sixty ships suddenly hoist sail and flee through the engaged lines. Plutarch writes that here "Antony showed to all the world that he was no longer actuated by the thoughts and motives of a commander or a man, or indeed by his own judgment at all" — "as if he had been born part of her, and must move with her wheresoever she went," he abandoned his fighting men to follow "her that had so well begun his ruin and would hereafter accomplish it" [2].

**Final betrayal and death.** After the defeat, Antony cried out "that Cleopatra had betrayed him to the enemies he had made for her sake" [3]. She then tricked him into believing she was dead, prompting his suicide attempt — a wound from which he ultimately died in her arms [3].

In his formal comparison with Demetrius, Plutarch crystallizes the judgment: while Demetrius incurred no harm from his licentiousness, "Antony procured his ruin by his marriage" to the "foreign and unlawful woman," and "Antony deserted others, and ran away while men were fighting for him at the risk of their lives" [9].

results.question: How does Arrian portray Alexander's bond with his soldiers?
results.ideal_answer: As intense mutual devotion that survived even open conflict. At the Hyphasis the army's silent, grieved resistance forced the invincible king to turn back; when he nearly died among the Malli, the troops wept, cheered, and crowded to touch his hands, knees, and clothes as he showed himself alive; and at Opis the mutiny dissolved when he called every Macedonian his kinsman, sealing the reconciliation with kisses and a vast common banquet.
results.answer:
Arrian portrays Alexander's bond with his soldiers as deeply personal, reciprocal, and rooted in shared hardship. Several episodes in the *Anabasis* illustrate this.

First, Arrian presents Alexander as a commander who shares his men's sufferings rather than standing apart from them. In his speech at Opis, Alexander challenges his troops to compare wounds: "whoever of you has wounds, let him strip and show them, and I will show mine in turn; for there is no part of my body, in front at any rate, remaining free from wounds" [1]. He also claims to eat no better than his men and to "sit up at night to watch for you, that you may be able to sleep" [5].

Second, Arrian shows Alexander personally tending to his soldiers after battle. After the victory at the Granicus, he "visited each man, looked at their wounds, and inquired how and in the performance of what duty they had received them, allowing them both to speak and brag of their own deeds" [4]. After Issus, though himself wounded in the thigh, he again visited the wounded, gave the slain a splendid burial, and "spoke with eulogy to those whom he himself had recognised performing any gallant deed" [8].

Third, Arrian dramatizes the soldiers' emotional dependence on Alexander through their reactions to his near-fatal wound among the Mallians. When a rumor spread that he had died, "there arose a sound of lamentation from the entire army" and the men became "spiritless" and "perplexed as to the man who was to become the leader" [6]. When he finally appeared to them alive, the army erupted in joy: "the men raised a cheer, lifting their hands, some towards the sky and others to the king himself. Many even shed involuntary tears at the unexpected sight" [7]. They crowded around him, "some touching his hands, others his knees, others only his clothes" [7].

Arrian does not, however, present this bond uncritically. He notes that Alexander's friends reproached him for exposing himself to danger like a common soldier rather than a general, and Arrian himself adds that "he had not sufficient self-control to keep aloof from danger" [7]. This suggests that while the bond was genuine and deeply felt on both sides, Arrian saw Alexander's recklessness as a flaw that endangered the very army that loved him.

results.question: How does Livy portray the struggle between patricians and plebeians?
results.ideal_answer: As the defining, recurring affliction of the early Republic — "the discord among the several orders... the bane of this city." Driven by debt and oppression, the commons twice seceded (the first ended by Menenius Agrippa's fable of the belly and the members, the second on the Sacred Mount against the decemvirs); each crisis extracted new concessions — tribunes, appeal, laws — while Livy moralizes on how hard moderation is when each side's pursuit of power or liberty makes it an object of dread to the other.
results.answer:
Livy portrays the struggle between patricians and plebeians as a protracted, bitter conflict marked by repeated plebeian secessions and grudging patrician concessions. He frames it as a contest over political rights and economic justice, with the plebeians using withdrawal from the city as their primary weapon and the patricians slowly yielding ground.

The first major episode is the secession to the Sacred Mount (traditionally 494 BC), which Livy recounts as a spontaneous withdrawal of the army without its consuls' orders, camping beyond the Anio and remaining peaceful but resolute [12]. This crisis directly produced the tribunate of the plebs — a concession extracted from the alarmed patricians [12].

Livy gives the patrician perspective a powerful voice in a speech by Appius Claudius (or a similar patrician spokesman), who laments the endless cycle of dissension: "What termination is there to be to our dissensions? when shall it be allowed us to have a united city?" He recites a litany of concessions — decemvirs created and abolished, tribunes restored, the right of appeal granted, and acts of the commons made binding on patricians — yet the plebeians still seize the Aventine and the Sacred Mount "against us" [3]. This speech reveals Livy's technique of dramatizing both sides: the patricians see themselves as endlessly yielding while the plebeians remain insatiable.

A second great secession occurs against the decemvirs, when the commons withdraw to the Sacred Mount again after the abuses of Appius Claudius and his colleagues. Livy describes the city left deserted, the forum empty but for a few old men, and patricians like Valerius and Horatius urging the senate to yield: "either we must not have the commons, or they must have their tribunes" [11][18]. The decemvirs are forced to resign, and the tribunician power is restored [14].

Livy also traces the incremental political gains: the law making plebiscites binding on the whole people, the opening of the consulship (via military tribunes with consular power), and eventually the Licinian-Sextian laws allowing plebeians to hold the consulship itself [17][13]. Throughout, he emphasizes that the patricians conceded each point only under duress, while the plebeians' leaders — Icilius, Duilius, Sicinius — are portrayed as skillful popular agitators [18][14].

In sum, Livy presents the Conflict of the Orders not as a simple morality tale but as a tense, often bitter political struggle in which both sides have legitimate grievances, the patricians resist change, and the plebeians achieve their aims through collective withdrawal and the threat of founding a separate city — a method Livy notes with admiration for its "moderation" in avoiding bloodshed [12][11].
results.judge_notes: faithfulness: The summary is firmly grounded in the provided Livy passages, but it wrongly attributes to Livy the detail that the plebeians threatened to found a separate city, which appears only in the Mommsen and Smith sources. | completeness: The answer thoroughly covers how Livy portrays the struggle by addressing the recurring secessions, incremental patrician concessions, narrative balance given to both sides through speeches, and Livy's moral themes of moderation and legitimate grievance on both sides, fully satisfying the question without needing every extra detail from the reference. | attribution: The answer correctly relies solely on Livy's passages to address the specific question about Livy's portrayal, with no conflicting sources to reconcile and no misattributions.

results.question: How did the Romans become a naval power during the First Punic War, according to Polybius?
results.ideal_answer: Through what Polybius calls extraordinary audacity. Having never thought of naval war and lacking even the knowledge to build quinqueremes, they used a wrecked Carthaginian ship as their model, trained rowers on benches set up on dry land, and invented the "crow" boarding-bridge that turned sea battles into land fights (first proven at Mylae); after catastrophic storm losses they persisted, rebuilt with lighter ships and trained crews, and won the decisive battle of Aegusa.
results.answer: According to Polybius, the Romans became a naval power during the First Punic War out of necessity. Seeing that the war was dragging on and that Carthage's naval superiority allowed it to raid Italy while Libya remained untouched, the Romans resolved to build a fleet of one hundred quinqueremes and twenty triremes [4]. They had no experience building quinqueremes, but a Carthaginian decked vessel ran aground and fell into their hands, serving as a model on which they constructed their entire fleet [4][1]. While the ships were being built, they trained crews by making the men sit on rowers' benches on dry land, arranged as they would be in actual vessels, with a Celeustes (time-keeper) in the middle to teach them to row in unison [1]. After a brief practice at sea, the fleet set out along the coast of Italy [1].
results.judge_notes: faithfulness: All claims—including Roman motivation, fleet size, the captured Carthaginian ship model, and dry-land training with a Celeustes—are grounded in cited passages [1] and [4]. | completeness: The answer concisely covers Polybius's core explanation—necessity, modeling ships on a captured Carthaginian vessel, and training rowers on dry land—fully addressing how Rome built its naval power without requiring every extra detail from the reference. | attribution: The sources agree and the answer accurately attributes the information to Polybius without any misattributions.

results.question: How does Thucydides describe the moral collapse caused by civil war?
results.ideal_answer: In his analysis of the Corcyra revolution, Thucydides argues that war is "a rough master" that levels men's characters to their fortunes. Words lost their ordinary meanings (recklessness became courage, moderation cowardice); religion was honored by neither side while fair phrases served guilty ends; the moderates perished between the factions; the "ancient simplicity" of honor was laughed out of existence and no man trusted his fellow. The concrete horror at Corcyra — fathers killing sons, suppliants slain at altars — set the pattern the whole Hellenic world then exceeded.
results.answer: Thucydides describes the moral collapse caused by civil war in his account of the Corcyraean revolution. He writes that words lost their ordinary meaning: reckless audacity was called loyal courage, prudent hesitation was deemed specious cowardice, moderation a cloak for unmanliness, and frantic violence the attribute of manliness. The advocate of extreme measures was always trusted, while his opponent was suspected. To succeed in a plot showed a shrewd head, to divine one even shrewder; but trying to avoid either was seen as breaking up one's party out of fear. Party loyalty came to outweigh even blood ties, with associations formed not for the blessings of established institutions but for their overthrow through ambition, and mutual trust rested on complicity in crime rather than religious sanction. Revenge was prized above self-preservation, and oaths of reconciliation held only until a better weapon came to hand; success by treachery won the palm of superior intelligence. The root cause, Thucydides says, was the lust for power arising from greed and ambition, from which proceeded the violence of the contending parties. Leaders on both sides used fair professions — political equality or moderate aristocracy — as covers for seeking their own prizes, recoiling from no means in their struggles and going to even greater lengths in vengeance than justice or the state's good demanded. Religion was honoured by neither party; using fair phrases to achieve guilty ends was in high repute. The moderate citizens perished between the two factions. The ancient simplicity in which honour had so largely entered was laughed down and disappeared; society split into camps where no man trusted his fellow, and neither promise nor oath commanded respect [2][3].
results.judge_notes: faithfulness: Every claim in the answer is directly supported by the cited Thucydides passages [2] and [3], with no invented or unsupported embellishment. | completeness: The answer thoroughly addresses every aspect of the question by detailing the corruption of language, the reversal of virtues, the destruction of trust and blood ties, the perishing of moderates, the hypocrisy of leaders, and the loss of religious and traditional values, without omitting anything explicitly asked for. | attribution: The sources agree on Thucydides' description of the moral collapse, and the answer accurately attributes all claims to Thucydides without any misattribution.

results.question: How does Thucydides contrast the Athenian and Spartan national characters?
results.ideal_answer: Largely through speeches. The Corinthians tell Sparta that the Athenians are addicted to innovation, swift in conception and execution, adventurous beyond their power, never at home — "born into the world to take no rest themselves and to give none to others" — while the Spartans keep what they have, invent nothing, and never go far enough. Archidamus answers by defending Spartan slowness as disciplined wisdom: warlike from self-control and honor, wise because educated too little to despise the laws.
results.answer:
Thucydides presents the most explicit contrast between the Athenian and Spartan national characters in the speech of the Corinthians at Sparta in Book 1. The Corinthians declare that the Spartans have little perception of "how widely, how absolutely different" the Athenians are from themselves, and then lay out a series of paired oppositions [1]:

- **Innovation vs. conservatism:** The Athenians are "addicted to innovation, and their designs are characterized by swiftness alike in conception and execution"; the Spartans have "a genius for keeping what you have got, accompanied by a total want of invention, and when forced to act you never go far enough."
- **Daring vs. caution:** Athenians are "adventurous beyond their power, and daring beyond their judgment, and in danger they are sanguine"; Spartans "attempt less than is justified by your power, to mistrust even what is sanctioned by your judgment, and to fancy that from danger there is no release."
- **Promptitude vs. procrastination:** "There is promptitude on their side against procrastination on yours."
- **Restlessness vs. stay-at-home:** The Athenians "are never at home, you are never from it: for they hope by their absence to extend their acquisitions, you fear by your advance to endanger what you have left behind."
- **Resilience:** Athenians are "swift to follow up a success, and slow to recoil from a reverse."
- **Self-sacrifice vs. self-preservation:** "Their bodies they spend ungrudgingly in their country's cause; their intellect they jealously husband to be employed in her service."

The Corinthians sum up the Athenian character as one that "toil[s] on in trouble and danger all the days of their life... their only idea of a holiday is to do what the occasion demands," concluding that "one might truly say that they were born into the world to take no rest themselves and to give none to others" [1].
results.judge_notes: faithfulness: All substantive claims and quotations are fully supported by passages [1] and [2], with no invented content; the only errors are citation misattributions, which are assessed separately and do not affect faithfulness. | completeness: The answer thoroughly identifies the mechanism (the Corinthians' speech in Book 1) and exhaustively details the paired oppositions contrasting Athenian and Spartan character, fully satisfying the question without needing the additional Archidamus material found in the reference. | attribution: The sources agree as they are all excerpts from the same work by Thucydides, and the answer correctly attributes the contrast to Thucydides and the Corinthians in prose without any misattribution.

results.question: What does Herodotus find most remarkable about Babylon?
results.ideal_answer: Its unmatched magnificence and its customs. He marvels at the city's scale — the deep moat and walls fifty royal cubits thick and two hundred high, and the temple of Zeus Belos with its eight stacked towers — and singles out two customs: the annual bride auction, where money from the beautiful maidens dowered the plain ones (in his opinion their wisest), and the carrying of the sick into the marketplace, since they use no physicians and every passer-by must offer advice.
results.answer: Herodotus describes Babylon's size and magnificence as surpassing all other known cities, calling it "a city such as I shall say" and stating that "it had a magnificence greater than all other cities of which we have knowledge" [4]. He details its immense walls — fifty royal cubits thick and two hundred cubits high — with a hundred bronze gates, and notes that the wall-top was broad enough to turn a four-horse chariot [4]. Beyond the city itself, the feature that most impresses him is the temple of Zeus Belos (Belus), which he describes as an eight-towered ziggurat with an external ascent, resting places, and a golden table in the topmost shrine [18]. He also singles out for special wonder the embankment-mounds along the Euphrates built by Queen Nitocris, calling them "worthy to cause wonder for its size and height" [19]. Thus, while Herodotus marvels at the whole city, its monumental architecture — particularly the temple-tower of Belus and the river embankments — stands out as most remarkable.
results.judge_notes: faithfulness: Every claim in the answer is directly supported by the provided passages, including the descriptions of Babylon's size and walls [4], the eight-tiered temple of Belos with its golden table [18], and Nitocris's wonder-worthy embankments [19], while Rawlinson [1] confirms Herodotus was chiefly struck by the city's extraordinary edifices. | completeness: The answer thoroughly covers Babylon's physical magnificence and architecture but omits the customs—the annual bride auction and the sick being carried to the marketplace for advice—that Herodotus equally singles out as remarkable. | attribution: The sources agree and the answer correctly attributes all details to Herodotus with accurate citations, requiring no disagreement-surfacing.

results.question: How does Suetonius chart Caligula's descent from popular prince to monster?
results.ideal_answer: Suetonius structures the Life around an explicit pivot: "Thus far we have spoken of him as a prince. What remains to be said of him, bespeaks him rather a monster than a man." His accession brought genuine joy — the soldiers' beloved camp-child, heir to Germanicus' virtues — though Tiberius had already predicted he was "rearing a hydra for the people of Rome." After the pivot comes the monster: claiming divine majesty, beheading the gods' statues to mount his own head, the temple to his own divinity, and cruelties without limit.
results.answer:
Suetonius charts Caligula's descent by structuring the biography around a single, explicit pivot. He first recounts the emperor's early reign as a beloved prince: the Roman people greeted his accession with ecstatic joy, calling him "their star," "their chick," and "their pretty puppet" [20]. Caligula initially courted popularity by recalling exiles, publicly burning the records of past trials, declaring he "had no ears for informers" [33], restoring suppressed writings, remitting taxes, and compensating victims of fire [32]. He also entertained the people lavishly with games, spectacles, and distributions of food [29].

Then Suetonius draws a sharp line: "Thus far we have spoken of him as a prince. What remains to be said of him, bespeaks him rather a monster than a man" [22]. After this turning-point, the narrative becomes a catalogue of savagery and madness. Caligula fed criminals to wild beasts, branded and sawed men asunder, and compelled parents to watch their sons' executions [5]. He boasted of his "inflexible rigour," told his grandmother "all things are lawful for me," and referred to signing execution lists as "clearing his accounts" [7]. He prolonged victims' suffering with the order "Strike so that he may feel himself die" and wished "the Roman people had but one neck" [3]. Suetonius also notes his physical and mental deterioration—epilepsy, insomnia, and a suspected love-potion-induced frenzy [21]—and his descent into rapacious plundering of the populace through false accusations and confiscations [38]. The biography thus moves from a hopeful, popular beginning to a systematic exposé of monstrosity, with the transition marked by Suetonius's own editorial declaration.
results.judge_notes: faithfulness: Every claim is directly supported by the provided passages, from the initial popular reception and early benefactions to the explicit pivot and subsequent catalog of cruelties, with no invented content. | completeness: The answer fully explains Suetonius’s structural pivot and richly documents both the beloved prince and the monster phases, completely addressing how the descent is charted. | attribution: The provided passages all come from the same author and agree on the narrative structure of Caligula's life, and the answer accurately attributes the details to Suetonius without any misattribution.

results.question: How does Tacitus characterize German marriage and the position of women?
results.ideal_answer: With pointed admiration. The marriage gifts — oxen, a bridled horse, shield, spear, and sword — admonish the wife that she comes as a partner in toils and dangers, "to suffer and to dare equally with him, in peace and in war... thus she is to live; thus to die." The Germans live "fenced around with chastity": adultery is extremely rare and instantly punished, no one calls seduction the way of the world, and in the strictest tribes a woman takes one husband "as one body and one life."
results.answer: Tacitus describes German marriage as strict, severe, and commendable above all other Germanic customs. They are almost the only barbarians content with one wife; a very few men of high rank take multiple wives not out of lust but because their alliance is sought on account of their nobility [5]. The wife does not bring a dowry to her husband but receives one from him — not ornaments but oxen, a caparisoned steed, a shield, spear, and sword — and she in turn gifts arms to her husband. This ceremonial exchange signifies that she is a partner in all toils and dangers, to suffer and dare equally with him in peace and war [5]. German women live fenced around with chastity, uncorrupted by seductive spectacles or convivial incitements, and adultery is extremely rare. When it occurs, the husband punishes instantly: he cuts off the adulteress’s hair, strips her, expels her from his house in front of her relations, and drives her through the village with stripes. No indulgence is shown to a prostitute; neither beauty, youth, nor wealth can gain her a husband [1]. In some states only virgins marry, and a wife’s hopes end at once; she takes one husband as she would one body and one life, loving him as her marriage itself [1]. To limit offspring or kill later-born children is deemed infamous, and their good habits carry more force than good laws elsewhere [4]. Children are raised without distinction between master and slave; every mother suckles her own children. Virgins are not hurried into marriage; both sexes unite equally matched and robust, and the children inherit their parents’ vigor [4]. In battle, women and children are near at hand, the most revered witnesses of each man’s conduct, their most liberal applauders. Warriors bring their wounds to their mothers and wives for relief, and the women fearlessly count and probe the gashes while providing food and encouragement [7]. Tradition holds that armies on the point of breaking have been rallied by women’s earnest supplications, their bodies placed in the way, and their vivid pictures of slavery, a calamity that Germans endure with greater impatience for their women than for themselves [7]. They even ascribe a certain sanctity and foresight to the female sex, neither despising their counsels nor disregarding their warnings [7]. Overall, Tacitus presents German women as chaste, robust, respected partners who share the hardships of men and are honored as the moral center of the family and tribe.

results.question: How does Suetonius portray the character of Tiberius?
results.ideal_answer: As a dissimulating tyrant whose vices, long imperfectly concealed, flowered once he withdrew to Capri. Even Augustus reportedly pitied the Roman people for being "ground by the jaws of such a slow devourer." The soldiers' nickname Biberius marked his drunkenness from youth; at Capri, "remote from the observation of the people of Rome, he abandoned himself to all the vicious propensities which he had long but imperfectly concealed"; and the translator's verdict is of an "execrable tyrant" whose indiscriminate cruelty spared neither age nor sex.
results.answer:
Suetonius portrays Tiberius as a deeply flawed and contradictory figure, whose outward moderation masked a cruel, suspicious, and debauched nature.

**Early dissimulation and affected modesty:** At the start of his reign, Tiberius cultivated an image of humility and restraint. He declined excessive honours, refused the title of emperor, and showed deference to the senate and magistrates [17]. He also displayed a strong aversion to flattery, rebuking those who addressed him as “lord” or called his occupations “sacred” [13].

**Fear and distrust:** Suetonius identifies fear and distrust as his most predominant characteristics. He never attended a banquet without a guard, had visitors searched, and was so alarmed by rumours of conspiracy that he considered abdicating [1].

**Cruelty:** After the death of his son Drusus and the fall of Sejanus, Tiberius abandoned all restraint and indulged in extreme cruelty. Suetonius writes that “not a day passed without the punishment of some person or other,” and he describes in detail the tortures Tiberius devised, such as inducing prisoners to drink heavily and then tying their genitals with harp-strings to prevent urination [4][2]. Even as a boy, his rhetoric teacher called him “mud mixed with blood” [7].

**Lack of paternal affection:** Tiberius showed no grief at the death of his own son Drusus and actively disparaged his adopted son Germanicus, whose death he was suspected of arranging [10].

**Vicious private life:** Once secluded on Capri, Tiberius gave free rein to his vices. Suetonius reports his excessive drinking (earning him the nickname “Biberius”), his creation of a pleasure-palace with lewd performances, and his sexual abuse of both women and youths [11][15][14][12].

**Physical appearance and mannerisms:** Suetonius also provides a physical portrait: Tiberius was tall and robust, with a handsome but pimpled face, a stiff neck, a frowning countenance, and a stammer. He notes that Augustus excused these traits as “natural defects, which proceeded from no viciousness of mind” [5][3].

In sum, Suetonius presents Tiberius as a tyrant whose early show of moderation was a facade, behind which lurked a suspicious, cruel, and debauched character.

results.question: How does Josephus portray the factional strife among the Jews that doomed Jerusalem?
results.ideal_answer: Josephus presents the fall of Jerusalem as a self-inflicted catastrophe of civil war within the walls. The zealots fractured into rival factions — Eleazar son of Simon making "the first separation of the zealots from the people" and seizing the inner temple, against John of Gischala and, in the city, Simon — until there were "three treacherous factions" fighting one another even as Titus besieged them. In that warfare they burned the storehouses, so that almost all the corn "which would have been sufficient for a siege of many years" was destroyed and the people were "taken by the means of the famine" their own leaders had prepared. The common people were "their prey on both sides," and Josephus moralizes that no age ever bred "a generation more fruitful in wickedness" — the Jews destroyed themselves.
results.answer: Josephus portrays the factional strife among the Jews as the primary cause of Jerusalem's destruction, even more than the Roman siege. He describes the sedition breaking into three factions—Eleazar, John, and Simon—who fought one another savagely even while the Romans encircled the city [1][9]. The factions burned the city's stores of grain and provisions, deliberately cutting off their own resources and ensuring that famine would defeat them when a siege might otherwise have been withstood for years [2]. The ordinary citizens, caught between the warring factions, were like a great body torn in pieces; they longed for the Romans to come and deliver them from their domestic miseries, yet the seditious leaders agreed only on killing anyone who inclined toward peace or desertion [3]. Josephus explicitly declares that "the sedition destroyed the city, and the Romans destroyed the sedition," and he calls the zealots' original attack on the people "the beginning of the city's destruction" [9][1]. In his view, the Jews brought their own ruin upon themselves through internal madness, making the Roman conquest almost an act of mercy in comparison.
results.judge_notes: faithfulness: Every claim in the answer is directly supported by the provided passages, including the three factions, the burning of grain stores, citizens wishing for Roman deliverance, the agreement to kill peace-seekers, and Josephus's explicit judgment that sedition destroyed the city. | completeness: The answer comprehensively covers Josephus's portrayal of the three warring factions, their destruction of the city's provisions, the resulting famine, the suffering of civilians, and his moral conclusion that the Jews brought ruin upon themselves. | attribution: The provided passages all agree as they are from the same author describing the same events, and the answer correctly attributes all claims to Josephus with accurate citation markers without any misattribution.

results.question: How does Josephus characterize the reign of Herod the Great?
results.ideal_answer: Josephus draws Herod as a king of dazzling success abroad and monstrous cruelty at home. He stood so high in Rome's favour that "he arrived at that pitch of felicity" of being, after Agrippa, the man Caesar valued most, and he crowned his reign with vast building works, "undertaking a very great work, that is, to build of himself the temple of God," alongside Caesarea and his fortresses. Yet the suspicion that secured his throne consumed his own household: he had his beloved wife tried and executed — "thus was Mariamne led to execution" — and later his sons. His end matched his cruelty: a loathsome disease in which "his privy-member was putrefied, and produced worms," which Josephus reads as divine punishment.
results.answer:
Josephus characterizes Herod the Great's reign as a study in contradictions driven by a single overriding passion: ambition for honor. In *Antiquities of the Jews*, he explicitly addresses those who "stand amazed at the diversity of Herod's nature," noting that when one considers his magnificence and benefits to mankind, one must confess he had a "nature vastly beneficent" [1]. Yet when one looks at the punishments he inflicted — not only on his subjects but on his nearest relations — one sees a "severe and unrelenting disposition" that was "brutish, and a stranger to all humanity" [1]. Josephus rejects the view that these were two different natures, arguing instead that both sprang from the same source: Herod was "a man ambitious of honor, and quite overcome by that passion" [1]. This ambition made him magnificent wherever there was hope of a future memorial, but because his expenses exceeded his abilities, he was "necessitated to be harsh to his subjects" to extract revenue, and he punished even his kindred and friends if they seemed to think of any innovation in his government, out of a desire "that he might be himself alone honored" [1].

This pattern recurs throughout Josephus's account. Herod undertook the rebuilding of the Temple as "the most glorious of all his actions" and "sufficient for an everlasting memorial of him" [2]. He built cities, fortresses, and palaces on a grand scale — including the fortress Herodium named after himself, with its costly palaces and marble steps [6] — and lavished gifts on foreign cities across the eastern Mediterranean, rebuilding temples, paving streets, and endowing games [9][10]. Yet this same "magnificent temper" led him to transgress Jewish customs, setting aside laws and building temples to idols in foreign territories, while apologizing that he acted only under Roman compulsion — though Josephus notes he "had himself entirely in view all the while" and was "very ambitious to leave great monuments of his government to posterity" [3]. He kept his people obedient through implacable punishment as much as through provident care in their distresses [3]. In sum, Josephus presents Herod as a ruler of extraordinary energy and generosity whose reign was ultimately defined by an insatiable ambition that made him both a great builder and benefactor, and a ruthless tyrant to his own people and family.

results.question: How does Tacitus portray the role of the armies in making and unmaking emperors in the civil war?
results.ideal_answer: Tacitus makes the legions the true power in the state. The convulsion of 69 laid bare "that secret of the empire, that emperors could be made elsewhere than at Rome" — sovereignty now belonged to whichever army would proclaim its general. That loyalty was bought: the city soldiery turned on Nero and were inclined to change "when they found that the donative promised in Galba's name was withheld," and under each claimant discipline dissolved — "Much however as Vitellius indulged his generals, his soldiers enjoyed yet greater licence." The praetorians embodied the menace, bursting in arms upon Otho's banquet and threatening the Senate. Throughout, emperors are made and unmade by the greed, licence, and violence of the soldiery.
results.answer:
Tacitus, in his *Histories*, portrays the armies—especially the legions of Germany and the eastern provinces—as the decisive force that both makes and unmakes emperors during the Year of the Four Emperors, often acting on impulse, self-interest, or the promptings of ambitious commanders rather than principle.

The armies' power to *unmake* an emperor is starkly illustrated when the legions of Lower and Upper Germany revolt against Galba. On January 1, the soldiers of the 1st and 5th legions are so mutinous that some throw stones at Galba's images, while the 4th and 18th legions break his images to pieces—all without any legate or tribune speaking for Galba [5]. Tacitus notes that "not a single legate or tribune exerted himself for Galba; some, as is usual in a tumult, were even conspicuously active in mutiny" [5]. Galba's downfall is sealed by his failure to offer a donative; Tacitus observes that "their feelings might have been conciliated by the very smallest liberality on the part of the parsimonious old man. He was ruined by his old-fashioned inflexibility, and by an excessive sternness" [6].

The same armies immediately proceed to *make* a new emperor in Vitellius. Tacitus describes how, after the mutiny, Fabius Valens entered the Colonia Agrippinensis with cavalry and "saluted Vitellius as Emperor"—and "all the legions belonging to the same province followed his example with prodigious zeal" [4]. The soldiers' enthusiasm is portrayed as driven by external prompting, personal enthusiasm, and even avarice, with whole companies offering their rations, belts, and silver-decorated arms [4]. Tacitus adds a damning aside about the army that paraded into Rome: "It was a glorious sight, and the army was worthy of a better Emperor than Vitellius" [3].

Vitellius is later *unmade* by the same dynamics. When the legions of the East proclaim Vespasian, Tacitus shows a few soldiers near Vespasian suddenly saluting him as Emperor, then "all the rest hurried up, called him Caesar and Augustus, and heaped on him all the titles of Imperial rank" [1]. Mucianus then administers the oath of allegiance to the eager soldiers [1]. Vitellius's own commander Caecina later assembles chosen centurions and soldiers, extols Vespasian, and makes them swear allegiance to him, tearing down Vitellius's images [7]. When the troops learn of this, they explode with rage—less from loyalty than from wounded pride at being handed over like captured goods without a fight [7]. After the battle of Cremona, the Gallic auxiliaries, who "served without attachment," promptly desert Vitellius [9].

Throughout, Tacitus emphasizes that the soldiers' allegiance is fickle, driven by immediate circumstances rather than principle. Men follow where others lead, "in obedience to that innate tendency of men, which makes them quick to follow where they are slow to lead" [5], and in mutinies, "the side of the majority became the side of all" [2]. Emperors are made and unmade not by senatorial decree or constitutional process, but by the shifting moods and material interests of the legionaries.

results.question: How does Xenophon portray the rule and downfall of the Thirty Tyrants at Athens?
results.ideal_answer: Xenophon shows the Thirty beginning with popular approval and ending in bloody tyranny and overthrow. At first they prosecuted the informers and base men of the democracy, and "the new senate gladly recorded its vote of condemnation against them"; but they soon turned to plunder and murder, passing a resolution "empowering each of the committee to seize on one of the resident aliens apiece, to put his victim to death, and to confiscate his property." Their rule grew so murderous that in eight months they had "slain almost more men than the Peloponnesians together in ten years of warfare." When Theramenes resisted Critias he was struck off the roll and "dragged through the Agora" to his death. Finally Thrasybulus and the exiles from Phyle prevailed, and the Athenians "reappointed the several magistrates; the constitution began to work afresh, and civic life was recommenced."
results.answer:
Xenophon portrays the rule of the Thirty Tyrants as a rapid descent into lawless tyranny and bloodshed, followed by a downfall brought on by internal discord and armed resistance. Initially, the Thirty were appointed to frame a new constitution, but instead of publishing laws, they appointed a compliant senate and magistrates [7]. They first prosecuted notorious informers, which won public approval, but soon sought absolute control by securing a Spartan garrison under Callibius with Lysander's assistance [7]. With this armed backing, they began arresting and executing not only the base but also respectable citizens who might oppose them [7][1].

Xenophon highlights the internal conflict between the moderate Theramenes and the bloodthirsty Critias. Critias argued that their rule required the same ruthless guarding as a tyranny, and the killings multiplied daily [1]. Theramenes objected to the indiscriminate slaughter and argued for broadening the government, leading the Thirty to create a list of Three Thousand citizens who alone would share power; everyone else was disarmed when the Spartan guards seized their weapons [1][6]. To fund their guards, the Thirty each seized a resident alien, executed him, and confiscated his property — an act Theramenes denounced as worse than the informers’ methods [2].

Theramenes’ opposition led to his trial before the senate. Critias struck him from the list of the Three Thousand, condemning him without a vote, and the Eleven dragged him from the altar of Hestia — a violation of sanctuary [3]. After his execution, the Thirty, “feeling that they had it in their power to play the tyrant without fear,” barred all not on the list from the city and pursued exiles even into the countryside, driving refugees to Megara and Thebes [5].

The downfall began when Thrasybulus seized the fortress of Phyle with about seventy followers. The Thirty, the Three Thousand, and the Knights marched out but failed to take the position; a heavy snowstorm forced their retreat [5]. Thrasybulus then descended on Piraeus at night with a thousand men. The Thirty advanced with their Spartan guards, cavalry, and hoplites, but at Munychia, Thrasybulus’s forces, though outnumbered, held a steep position. He reminded his men that the Thirty had robbed them of their city and proscribed their friends, and that the gods were on their side [8]. In the ensuing battle, the Thirty were defeated; Critias himself fell [8]. Xenophon thus presents the Thirty’s rule as a reign of arbitrary terror and greed, undone by internal faction (the destruction of Theramenes) and the armed resurgence of exiled democrats led by Thrasybulus.

results.question: How does Strabo portray the decayed condition of the once-great cities of Greece in his own day?
results.ideal_answer: Strabo repeatedly registers how the once-great cities of Greece had dwindled or vanished by his own day. In the Argolid "some of the cities mentioned in the Catalogue of the Ships, and said to be subject to Argos, have disappeared." In Arcadia the famous cities — Mantinea, Orchomenus, Heraea, Cleitor, Pheneus, Stymphalus and the rest — "either exist no longer, or traces and signs only of their existence are visible," the country sunk into a deserted condition. Lesser towns such as Dyspontium "was razed, and the greatest part of the inhabitants removed" abroad. Even Corinth had been sacked, Strabo recalling Polybius's eyewitness account of the soldiers' indifference to its art and sacred offerings. The recurring note is depopulation and ruin set against a glorious past.
